proposals.title: Coherence vs. Utility: A Socially-Aware Evaluation of Topic Models Across Languages and Annotator Backgrounds
proposals.motivation: Contextual topic models (e.g., BERTopic, Top2Vec) are evaluated primarily on automated coherence metrics (e.g., NPMI), which assume coherence equals quality. However, this assumption is untested for real-world tasks where human judgments of usefulness are paramount, such as content moderation or social science analysis. This gap is critical, as models optimized for coherence may produce topics that are statistically neat but pragmatically unhelpful or even misleading for downstream applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there is a significant divergence between automated coherence scores and human judgments of topic usefulness, and that this divergence is systematically influenced by annotator demographics (e.g., gender, L2 status) and the language of the text.
proposals.method: We will generate topics from multilingual social media datasets using BERTopic and compare them against topics from classical LDA. For evaluation, we will conduct a large-scale annotation study, recruiting annotators with varied gender and language backgrounds. Annotators will rate topics on dimensions of usefulness, interpretability, and actionability for a hate speech monitoring task. We will measure intra-annotator agreement and stability over time, adapting methods from Hovy's prior work, and use statistical modeling (e.g., mixed-effects regression) to quantify the gap between automated coherence (NPMI) and human ratings, and the moderating effects of social factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical framework for evaluating topic models based on socially-grounded utility rather than abstract coherence. It will yield concrete guidelines for model selection in sensitive NLP tasks and contribute a novel, annotated multilingual dataset linking topic quality to annotator background.
proposals.novelty: While coherence metrics are standard, directly challenging their equivalence to usefulness with a systematic, socially-informed annotation protocol is novel. Integrating the analysis of annotator demographics and language into topic model evaluation bridges the gap between technical NLP and sociolinguistic perspectives.

proposals.title: Beyond Social Media: Assessing the Robustness of Socially Aware NLP Methods on Technical and Short-Text Corpora
proposals.motivation: Current research on socially aware NLP, including bias and hate speech detection, is heavily benchmarked on general social media or news corpora. This leaves a critical gap: we do not know if these methods generalize to specialized domains like scientific literature or technical forums, where language use, bias manifestations, and annotator expectations differ significantly. Testing on such corpora is essential for deploying fair and robust language technologies in professional and scientific contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that state-of-the-art methods for detecting social biases and hate speech will exhibit significant performance degradation and unstable annotator agreement when applied to highly technical or short-text scientific datasets, due to domain-specific jargon, implicit bias, and context sparsity.
proposals.method: We will adapt the annotator-centric, systems-level analysis from Hovy's prior work on hate speech labeling and gender gaps in speech recognition. First, we will curate or adapt existing technical corpora (e.g., arXiv abstracts, StackExchange posts) and short-text datasets (e.g., scientific figure captions, patent claims). We will then apply and fine-tune pre-2025 transformer-based models (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa) for relevant tasks (e.g., toxicity detection, gender bias probing). Crucially, we will conduct a detailed annotator study to measure intra-annotator agreement and stability within these specialized domains, analyzing disagreements through the lens of domain expertise and linguistic register.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evaluation of socially aware NLP methods in technical and scientific domains, identifying failure modes and domain-specific challenges. It will yield practical guidelines for adapting bias mitigation and content moderation systems beyond social media, contributing to more equitable language technologies in academic and professional settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work tests social awareness on mainstream data, this proposal explicitly shifts the focus to specialized, high-stakes domains where NLP is increasingly deployed but bias is poorly understood. It uniquely combines the analysis of model robustness with an in-depth, replicable study of annotator behavior in these novel contexts.

proposals.title: Distilling Social Context: Efficient and Fair Topic Models for Multilingual Hate Speech Detection
proposals.motivation: Large, multilingual contextual models (e.g., BERT, XLM-R) capture nuanced social and topical cues crucial for tasks like hate speech detection, but their computational cost hinders deployment in real-time or resource-constrained environments. This creates a trade-off between model performance and accessibility, potentially excluding researchers and practitioners who need efficient, socially-aware tools. Knowledge distillation offers a path to efficiency, but its impact on fairness and stability across languages and demographic groups remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: A smaller model distilled from a large multilingual topic model can retain not only task performance but also the larger model's sensitivity to social and topical context, thereby mitigating performance disparities (e.g., gender gaps) observed in compressed models for speech and language tasks.
proposals.method: We will distill a large, pre-trained multilingual model (e.g., XLM-R) fine-tuned for hate speech detection with contextual topic features into a compact Transformer (e.g., DistilBERT) or LSTM student. Using task-specific distillation (logit matching) and intermediate layer mimicry (e.g., attention or hidden state transfer), we will train the student to replicate the teacher's predictions and internal representations. We will rigorously evaluate the distilled model's efficiency, accuracy, and fairness across languages and speaker demographics, analyzing performance gaps as in Hovy's prior work on speech recognition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, efficient model for multilingual social NLP tasks while explicitly evaluating the social costs of compression. It contributes a framework for socially-aware distillation, moving beyond mere accuracy preservation to assess the retention of nuanced contextual understanding critical for equitable NLP systems.
proposals.novelty: While distillation for efficiency is common, this proposal uniquely integrates Hovy's focus on social factors (gender, language, annotator stability) into the distillation objective and evaluation, asking whether social and topical awareness can be compressed without exacerbating performance disparities.

proposals.title: Distributed Training for Socially Aware Language Models on Petabyte-Scale Web Corpora
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) are trained on vast web corpora, but their training pipelines are often opaque and not designed to systematically investigate the social biases encoded at scale. As models grow, the computational and methodological challenges of training on petabyte-scale data while maintaining analytical control over social variables (e.g., gender, language, hate speech) become critical bottlenecks for responsible NLP. This work addresses the need for scalable, socially-informed training frameworks that can handle web-scale data while enabling granular analysis of model behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distributed training framework with integrated, lightweight social metadata tracking will reveal that performance gaps and bias amplification in multilingual models are non-linear functions of data scale and composition, not merely of model size.
proposals.method: We will develop a distributed training system using the Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP) technique and pipeline parallelism (e.g., with PyTorch or DeepSpeed) to efficiently train transformer-based models on petabytes of filtered Common Crawl data. Crucially, we will integrate a persistent metadata layer that logs provenance (domain, inferred language, timestamp) and predicted social attributes (using lightweight, pre-2026 classifiers for gender association and hate speech likelihood) for each training batch. This allows for correlating training data characteristics with emergent model performance evaluated on controlled tasks, extending methods from Hovy's prior work on gender gaps in ASR and annotator stability.
proposals.contribution: This project will contribute 1) an open-source, scalable training framework with built-in social metadata logging, and 2) a large-scale empirical study mapping the relationship between web-scale data composition and the emergence of social biases in multilingual LLMs. It will provide concrete methodologies for auditing training data at scale.
proposals.novelty: While distributed training for LLMs is established, the novel integration of continuous, fine-grained social metadata tracking during petabyte-scale training is unexplored. This moves beyond post-hoc analysis to enable a causal, longitudinal study of bias formation during training.

proposals.title: Controlled Synthetic Document Generation for Socially Aware Data Augmentation in Low-Resource Tasks
proposals.motivation: Data scarcity and annotation bias are critical bottlenecks in socially aware NLP, particularly for tasks like hate speech detection or gender bias analysis in low-resource languages. Existing data augmentation techniques often fail to preserve the nuanced socio-linguistic properties (e.g., dialect, gender markers, hate speech intensity) of the original data, limiting their utility for robust model training. Generating coherent, attribute-controlled synthetic documents could mitigate these issues by expanding and balancing training sets while maintaining social realism.
proposals.hypothesis: A topic-embedding model, conditioned on socio-demographic and linguistic attributes (e.g., perceived gender, language variety, hate speech label), can generate coherent and stylistically consistent synthetic documents that effectively augment training data for socially sensitive NLP tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend a neural topic model (e.g., Contextualized Topic Model) or a controllable language model (e.g., CTRL, PPLM) to act as a generator. The model will be trained on existing annotated corpora (e.g., hate speech datasets, multilingual speech transcripts) to learn joint representations of content (topics) and social attributes (from annotator metadata). For generation, we will sample from the learned latent space, conditioning on specific attribute vectors (e.g., gender, language, label) to produce synthetic documents. Coherence and attribute fidelity will be evaluated using automated metrics (e.g., topic coherence, classifier-based verification) and human evaluation with a focus on annotator agreement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel data augmentation framework for socially aware NLP, specifically targeting low-resource scenarios and bias mitigation. It will yield a publicly available tool for generating attribute-controlled text and empirical evidence on the efficacy of synthetic data for improving model fairness and robustness in tasks like hate speech detection and multilingual ASR evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While topic models and language models have been used for generation, their application is novel for the *controlled synthesis* of documents with specific socio-linguistic attributes to address data scarcity in socially sensitive domains. This directly inverts the typical analytical focus of Hovy's prior work on measuring biases to proactively generating data to combat them.

proposals.title: Topic-Aware Document Retrieval via Contextual Embedding Alignment: A Study on Socially Salient Categories
proposals.motivation: Current document retrieval systems often rely on keyword matching or static topic models, which struggle with the nuanced, context-dependent expression of socially salient concepts like hate speech or gendered language. This limits the ability to systematically study how these phenomena manifest across different corpora and languages, a core challenge in socially aware NLP. A method to directly map high-level thematic descriptions to dense contextual representations would enable more precise and flexible corpus analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual-encoder architecture, trained to align a topic description encoder with a document encoder, can effectively rank documents by their semantic relevance to abstract, socially-grounded topics, outperforming keyword-based and classical topic model baselines.
proposals.method: We will develop a contrastive learning framework. For a given topic (e.g., 'covert gendered hate speech'), we will generate descriptive text prompts. A pre-trained language model (e.g., BERT, XLM-R) will encode these prompts. A separate, potentially frozen, encoder will generate contextual embeddings for candidate documents. We will train the topic encoder using a multiple-negatives ranking loss (like InfoNCE) on annotated datasets (e.g., hate speech corpora), forcing the topic and relevant document embeddings to be close in a shared semantic space. Evaluation will involve ranking tasks on held-out social computing datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel retrieval paradigm that bridges high-level thematic analysis with dense representation search, specifically tailored for social NLP tasks. It will provide a reusable methodological tool for researchers to audit corpora or models for the presence of complex social categories, directly supporting work on fairness and bias.
proposals.novelty: While dual-encoders are used for QA, we adapt them to map abstract, researcher-defined topics—not just concrete queries—to document embeddings. This moves beyond supervised classification or unsupervised topic modeling to enable open-vocabulary, prompt-based retrieval for social concepts.

proposals.title: Non-Parametric Topic Modeling for Socially Aware Language Analysis: A Data-Adaptive Approach to Hate Speech and Gender Bias
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 topic models like LDA require a fixed, pre-specified number of topics (K), which is a poor fit for dynamic, user-generated corpora where the thematic scope evolves with data size and social context. This limitation is critical for analyzing hate speech and gender bias across languages, as rigid models may conflate distinct social phenomena or miss emerging discourses, undermining the reliability of downstream systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A non-parametric Bayesian topic model, where the number of latent topics grows sublinearly with corpus size, will more accurately and granularly capture the thematic structure of multilingual social media data, leading to improved characterization of hate speech categories and gender-related discourse patterns.
proposals.method: We will develop a model based on the Hierarchical Dirichlet Process (HDP), a pre-2026 non-parametric Bayesian method that automatically infers the number of topics from data. The core innovation will be integrating social and annotator metadata (inspired by Hovy's prior work) as covariates within the HDP framework, allowing topic distributions to shift based on language, annotator demographics, or temporal batch. We will validate the model on existing multilingual hate speech and speech recognition gender-bias corpora, evaluating topic quality, stability, and their correlation with performance gaps.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a flexible, data-adaptive framework for topic discovery in socially sensitive NLP tasks, moving beyond the arbitrary choice of K. It will contribute a methodological advance for analyzing how hate speech and gender bias manifest across different languages and communities, offering more nuanced insights for developing socially aware language technologies.
proposals.novelty: While HDP is known, its application is novel for dynamically scaling topic complexity specifically to model the evolving and socially grounded phenomena of hate and gender bias, tightly integrating the model's growth with annotator disagreement and cross-lingual variation analysis.

proposals.title: Latent Belief Activation: Modeling Context-Specific Annotator Bias from Mental State Language
proposals.motivation: Current NLP models treat annotator subjectivity as noise or correlate it with coarse demographics, obscuring the fine-grained, contextual beliefs that drive labeling decisions. This is critical for socially aware language technologies, as shown by work on hate speech annotation instability and gender performance gaps in speech models, where systematic bias arises from interpretative differences. A mechanistic account of how specific belief configurations are activated in different contexts is needed to move beyond demographic proxies and genuinely debias annotation processes and downstream models.
proposals.hypothesis: An annotator's latent belief system is not monolithic but consists of separable, stable dimensions (e.g., individualism, harm sensitivity) whose contextual activation can be predicted from their use of mental state language, and these activated belief states systematically predict their labeling behavior on subjective tasks like hate speech detection.
proposals.method: We will collect a new dataset where annotators label hate speech and emotion in texts, and also answer open-ended survey prompts eliciting mental state language (e.g., "Describe a time you felt offended"). Using pre-2026 transformer-based sentence embeddings (e.g., from Sentence-BERT) and topic modeling (e.g., neural topic models), we will extract belief-relevant language features. We will then train a multi-task model with a variational autoencoder (VAE) to learn a latent belief space, regularized for stability across prompts but allowing for context-specific activation, and use this latent representation to predict annotation labels via a downstream classifier.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a computational framework for modeling the causal-like link between contextual belief states and annotation bias, offering a more nuanced tool for debiasing datasets than demographic adjustment. It will advance the understanding of cultural cognition in NLP by showing how interpretative frameworks are dynamically constructed, directly informing practices for creating more reliable and socially aware language technologies.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the standard predictive direction by using language to infer latent, contextual belief states of the annotator, rather than using demographics to predict mental states of text authors. It explicitly models beliefs as a stable-yet-contextually-activated set of dimensions, moving beyond the assumption of fixed ideological profiles prevalent in pre-2026 work on annotator bias.

proposals.title: Modeling the Demographic Landscape of Normative Judgment: A Collective Annotation Framework for Mental State Classification
proposals.motivation: Current NLP models for mental state detection (e.g., emotion, distress) rely on datasets annotated under a single, researcher-defined normative standard, which risks encoding the biases of a narrow demographic. This is critical as pre-2026 work in hate speech and speech recognition has shown systematic performance gaps linked to gender and annotator background. Building socially aware language technologies requires understanding how normative judgments are themselves shaped by demographic perspective.
proposals.hypothesis: The collective, demographically-grounded prescription for a mental state label is a predictable function of the annotator panel's composition, and models trained to predict both an individual's state and the panel's normative judgment will more accurately capture societal plurality and reduce systematic bias.
proposals.method: We will collect a novel dataset where diverse annotators, characterized by a rich vector of verified demographics (ethnicity, SES, cultural background, education, profession), collectively define labels for mental state utterances via iterative discussion or consensus interfaces. We will train a multi-task model architecture (e.g., based on transformer encoders like BERT or XLM-R) with two heads: one to predict the individual's self-reported state (if available) or a distilled 'ground truth', and another to predict the annotator panel's collective judgment, using the panel's aggregated demographic vector as an auxiliary input. This will allow us to analyze the learned demographic coefficients.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce 1) a novel dataset and annotation protocol for demographically-aware mental state modeling, 2) a model that explicitly represents how normative judgments vary with demographic perspective, and 3) a framework for auditing and mitigating normative bias in affective computing systems, extending Hovy's prior work on annotator agreement and gender gaps.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the standard annotation paradigm by making the 'prescriptive' label an emergent, demographically-grounded property of the annotator collective, rather than a fixed gold standard, and explicitly models this variance within the learning algorithm.

proposals.title: Stratifying Subjectivity: A Two-Stage Annotation Framework to Isolate Objective Textual Segmentation from Subjective Labeling
proposals.motivation: Research on annotator subjectivity in NLP (e.g., for hate speech or sentiment) often treats the entire annotation process as inherently subjective, conflating the identification of a text's structural units with the labeling of those units. This conflation obscures the sources of disagreement and limits our ability to create more reliable datasets. By failing to separate objective linguistic segmentation from subjective interpretation, we miss opportunities to improve annotation protocols and model robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: The primary locus of annotator subjectivity in text annotation tasks lies in the application of subjective labels, not in the foundational, rule-based segmentation of the text into discrete propositional units. Forcing a decomposition of the annotation pipeline will significantly reduce variance in the segmentation stage compared to the labeling stage.
proposals.method: We will design a two-stage annotation protocol. First, annotators will segment texts (e.g., from social media or product reviews) into minimal propositional units using a strict, rule-based guideline inspired by syntactic and discourse segmentation principles (e.g., identifying independent clauses). This stage will use pre-2026 tools like spaCy for dependency parsing as a consistent reference. Second, annotators will apply subjective labels (e.g., hate speech intensity, sentiment polarity) only to these pre-segmented units. We will measure inter-annotator agreement (Fleiss' Kappa, Krippendorff's Alpha) separately for segmentation and labeling across multiple languages and tasks, analyzing the variance introduced at each stage.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for decomposing annotation subjectivity, offering clearer diagnostics for disagreement sources. It will challenge the field's focus on annotator bias by demonstrating that a core component of annotation can be objectified. The resulting protocols can lead to more consistent dataset creation, especially for socially aware language technologies dealing with hate speech and gender.
proposals.novelty: The stratigraphic analogy provides a non-obvious conceptual shift, treating text as an objective sequence to be segmented before subjective interpretation, unlike prior work which treats subjectivity as permeating the entire process. This formal, forced decomposition of the annotation pipeline is a novel operationalization of this distinction.

proposals.title: Beyond the Label: Quantifying Annotator Subjectivity in Text Segmentation for Hate Speech Detection
proposals.motivation: Current research on subjectivity and bias in NLP annotation, including Hovy's own work on intra-annotator agreement, focuses almost exclusively on label assignment. However, the foundational step of how annotators interpret and segment the input text itself—what constitutes the relevant unit for judgment—remains unexamined. This is a critical gap, as systematic differences in text segmentation, potentially influenced by annotator demographics or language background, could introduce upstream bias that propagates through model training and evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that annotator subjectivity manifests not only in label choice but also in the prior, implicit segmentation of the text, and that this segmentation variance correlates with annotator characteristics (e.g., gender, L1/L2 status) and explains a significant portion of downstream labeling disagreement.
proposals.method: We will design a novel annotation task where participants first highlight the specific spans of text they consider most salient for a hate speech judgment before assigning a label. Using a dataset of social media posts in English and German, we will recruit a demographically balanced pool of annotators. We will analyze segmentation patterns using metrics like Jaccard similarity and clustering, then model the relationship between segment choice, annotator metadata, and final label using multilevel regression. We will further train BERT-based models to predict if segmentation came from a specific annotator group.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical framework for measuring text segmentation subjectivity in NLP annotation. It will quantify a previously hidden source of dataset bias and offer new insights into the roots of annotator disagreement. The findings will directly inform more nuanced annotation protocols and bias mitigation strategies for language technologies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats the input text as a fixed, objective given, this proposal explicitly challenges that assumption, shifting the focus of subjectivity analysis from the output label to the input interpretation stage, a fundamental but overlooked dimension.

proposals.title: Disentangling Beliefs from Biases: A Meta-Learning Framework for Few-Shot Modeling of Annotator Rationales in Subjective NLP Tasks
proposals.motivation: Annotation for subjective NLP tasks like hate speech detection is plagued by low intra- and inter-annotator agreement, as shown in Hovy's prior work. This stems from annotators applying complex, often implicit belief systems. Current models either ignore this nuance, leading to biased systems, or require vast amounts of labeled data per annotator, which is impractical. A method to efficiently and robustly model an individual's judgment principles from minimal data is crucial for building socially aware, personalized language technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: Annotator rationales for subjective judgments can be meta-learned into a set of disentangled, reusable belief principles, and that actively querying the annotator with counterfactual scenarios generated from this model will significantly improve the model's generalization to novel, complex cases beyond the initial few-shot examples.
proposals.method: We will extend Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) to operate on structured rationale-label pairs. For each annotator, we collect few-shot judgments with accompanying free-text rationales, which are encoded using a pre-trained language model (e.g., BERT). The inner loop of MAML adapts to the annotator's specific examples, while the outer loop learns to initialize a model that can quickly distill rationales into a latent space of core belief vectors, disentangled from surface features via an adversarial discriminator. The system will then use this belief model to generate contrastive query examples (e.g., via controlled text generation) to probe the annotator's decision boundaries for refinement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for sample-efficient, personalized annotator modeling, directly addressing the stability issues in subjective labeling. It contributes a new benchmark for evaluating belief generalization in NLP and offers a concrete method to audit and mitigate systematic biases in annotation pipelines by making individual belief systems explicit and testable.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses rationales for model explanation or gathers them for aggregation, this proposal uniquely uses them in a meta-learning setup to *learn to learn* a disentangled belief system, combined with an active, counterfactual-driven refinement loop. This moves beyond modeling surface-level agreement to modeling the underlying cognitive principles of judgment.

proposals.title: Modeling the Annotator: Few-Shot Belief Learning for De-biasing Noisy Self-Reported Labels
proposals.motivation: Social NLP tasks like hate speech detection or mental health assessment rely on inherently subjective, self-reported labels, which are noisy proxies for underlying constructs. Annotator disagreement is systematic, reflecting individual belief systems and biases, not random error. Current multi-annotator modeling often requires substantial per-annotator data, failing in few-shot scenarios common with sensitive data or rare demographics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an individual's labeling behavior for subjective tasks can be modeled as a consistent, learnable mapping from their internal state to a label, and that inferring this personal belief model from few examples will allow for effective de-biasing of their labels, improving model generalization to ground truth.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage, meta-learning framework. First, we adapt Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) to learn a prior over belief models from a population of annotators, enabling rapid personalization. For a new annotator, their few labels fine-tune this prior into a personalized 'belief network' that predicts their label distribution given input text. Second, a downstream task model is trained not on raw labels, but on samples from the personalized belief network's posterior, effectively learning from de-biased, annotator-corrected signals. We will evaluate on existing multi-annotator hate speech datasets and simulated mental health self-reports.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical method for robust learning from noisy, subjective data with minimal calibration. It contributes a novel perspective on annotator modeling as few-shot belief learning, bridging social science concepts with meta-learning. The outcome is a framework that improves fairness and accuracy in tasks plagued by label subjectivity, directly addressing issues of performance gaps across groups.
proposals.novelty: While multi-annotator modeling exists, our approach uniquely frames it as few-shot belief learning, using meta-learning to personalize noise correction with very limited data, moving beyond simple aggregation or bias-addition models common pre-2026.

proposals.title: Generating Ideologically Aligned Text via Probabilistic Annotation Models: Simulating the Strategic Application of Hate Speech Norms
proposals.motivation: Current hate speech detection models often fail to account for the real-world inconsistency and strategic application of labels within specific belief communities. This leads to brittle systems that are easily gamed and poorly simulate actual ideological discourse. Understanding this nuanced, probabilistic labeling behavior is crucial for building more robust NLP systems and for analyzing how communities weaponize or shield themselves with their own prescriptive rules.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the annotation behavior of a belief community for a concept like hate speech can be modeled as a conditional probability distribution over labels, and that this model can be inverted to generate text that a specific community would probabilistically label in a targeted way, thereby capturing its strategic rhetorical patterns.
proposals.method: We will first construct a probabilistic annotation model (PAM) for a target community (e.g., a political subcommunity on Reddit) by extending methods from Hovy's work on intra-annotator agreement. Using data from platforms like Gab or Reddit, we will train a model (e.g., a Bayesian Neural Network or a transformer with Monte Carlo dropout) to predict a distribution of possible labels (hate/not-hate) for a given text, conditioned on contextual factors like topic and rhetorical framing. We will then invert this PAM using adversarial generation techniques (e.g., a GAN or a reinforcement learning policy guided by the PAM's output distribution) to produce novel text that maximizes the probability of a desired, strategically ambiguous label from the community's perspective.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for modeling and generating ideologically situated language, moving beyond deterministic classification. It will provide a tool for stress-testing hate speech detectors against realistic, community-specific adversarial examples and offer a new lens for computational social science to analyze the flexible application of normative labels in discourse.
proposals.novelty: While prior work models annotation disagreement as noise, we treat it as a signal of strategic community behavior and explicitly invert it for text generation. This combines social annotation modeling with adversarial generation in a way that directly addresses the gap between prescriptive rules and their probabilistic application in real communities.

proposals.title: Inverse Text Generation for Prescriptive Belief Systems: An Inverse Problem Framework for Controlled Language Production
proposals.motivation: Current text generation is primarily descriptive, trained on existing corpora that reflect and perpetuate societal biases. To create equitable language technologies, we need methods to generate text that explicitly conforms to a target normative or prescriptive belief system, such as non-hateful or gender-fair language. This requires inverting the annotation process, moving from a desired label to the text that elicits it, a challenge analogous to reconstructing a source image from sensor data in medical imaging.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing prescriptive text annotation as a forward model, we can treat the generation of text for a target belief label as an inverse problem that can be solved using iterative optimization or end-to-end training of a conditional generative model.
proposals.method: We will first define and train a robust 'forward model' classifier, using pre-2026 methods like BERT or RoBERTa, to map text to belief system labels (e.g., hate/non-hate, gendered/neutral). This model, informed by research on annotator disagreement and stability, will simulate a prescriptive labeling function. We will then train a conditional language model (e.g., a T5 or GPT-2 variant) where the conditioning signal is the error between the forward model's prediction for generated text and the target label. This will be optimized using gradient-based iterative refinement or a fully differentiable pipeline inspired by learned iterative reconstruction (LIR) techniques from imaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled framework for controlled text generation aligned with explicit normative goals, moving beyond keyword filtering or post-hoc correction. It will yield a scalable method to produce large, synthetic corpora for data augmentation that adhere to specific belief systems, directly addressing performance gaps in tasks like hate speech detection and gender-biased ASR.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the conceptual and technical adaptation of the inverse problem framework from medical imaging to linguistic belief systems, treating annotation as a forward model to be inverted, rather than relying on direct supervision or reinforcement learning from human feedback.

proposals.title: Decomposing Prescriptive Labels: A Latent Belief Space Model for Auditing Subjective Annotation Protocols
proposals.motivation: The creation of datasets for socially aware NLP tasks like hate speech detection relies on prescriptive labeling guidelines that collapse complex, multi-dimensional human judgments into single labels. This process, while necessary for task definition, obscures the underlying belief systems of annotators and guideline designers, making it difficult to audit what subjective trade-offs (e.g., between intent and harm) were enforced and what information was lost. This opacity hinders the diagnosis of systematic label bias and limits our understanding of how different cultural or ideological priors shape foundational training data.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed prescriptive labels for subjective tasks can be rationalized by a constrained, low-dimensional latent space of interacting belief axes (e.g., fairness, harm, intent, offensiveness), and this latent belief space can be probabilistically inferred from the collapsed labels alone.
proposals.method: We will treat the labeling process as a probabilistic graphical model where a latent multi-dimensional belief vector for each annotator-guideline pair generates the observed prescriptive label via a decision function. Using variational inference, we will learn the posterior distribution over these latent dimensions from existing labeled datasets (e.g., hate speech, toxicity). The model will be trained to reconstruct observed labels while encouraging sparsity and interpretability of the latent dimensions, which can be validated via targeted re-annotation studies probing specific belief trade-offs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel diagnostic tool for dataset creators to audit what their labeling protocols prioritize and suppress. It will enable the quantification of 'subjectivity loss' during dataset construction and allow for simulations of how label distributions would change under different belief system configurations, directly addressing issues of fairness and stability in annotation.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond inferring a single scalar bias or difficulty, we propose to recover the entire, interacting belief subspace that rationalizes prescriptive outcomes, a form of inverse modeling for annotation sociology that is not addressed by standard item-response theory or annotator modeling.

proposals.title: The Geography of Harm: A Multilingual, Multi-Model Evaluation of Stereotype Propagation in Language Models Using HONEST
proposals.motivation: The HONEST metric (Nangia et al., 2021) provides a crucial framework for measuring hurtful sentence completions in language models, but its initial application was limited to six European languages. As multilingual LMs are deployed globally, it is imperative to understand their potential to propagate harmful stereotypes across a wider range of typologically and culturally diverse languages. This expansion is necessary to ensure equitable and safe NLP technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance gap in stereotype propagation between monolingual and multilingual models, as well as the specific stereotype categories triggered, will vary significantly across language families and socio-cultural contexts, revealing systematic biases in current multilingual training data and architectures.
proposals.method: We will extend the HONEST evaluation framework to at least 30 languages, covering diverse families (e.g., Afro-Asiatic, Dravidian, Sino-Tibetan) and resource scenarios. Using the original template-based methodology, we will create and validate templates for new languages via native speaker annotators, analyzing inter-annotator agreement. We will then evaluate a suite of pre-2026 models (e.g., mT5, XLM-R, multilingual BERT variants) and compare results across languages, model sizes, and training data composition using statistical analysis to identify correlating factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale, cross-linguistic benchmark for stereotype evaluation in LMs, providing actionable insights for model developers. It will contribute a methodological framework for culturally-aware metric adaptation and deliver a detailed audit of widely used multilingual models, informing more socially responsible NLP development.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined gender or hate speech gaps in multilingual settings, this study systematically applies a structured stereotype metric (HONEST) at an unprecedented scale and linguistic diversity, moving beyond Euro-centric evaluations to uncover how harm manifests in under-studied languages.

proposals.title: Beyond Sentence Completion: Measuring Social Bias in Dialogue and Summarization with HONEST
proposals.motivation: The HONEST (Hurtful Sentence Completion Evaluation) framework provides a valuable, template-based method for quantifying hurtful completions in language models. However, its application is currently limited to sentence completion tasks, while real-world NLP systems are increasingly deployed in more complex generative settings like dialogue and summarization. These tasks involve different model behaviors (e.g., conversational history, source compression) that may introduce or amplify social biases in ways not captured by current evaluations, creating a critical gap in responsible AI assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that (1) bias metrics derived from the HONEST framework can be effectively adapted to dialogue and summarization tasks, and (2) these adapted metrics will reveal significant, task-specific bias profiles that differ from those observed in sentence completion, correlating with annotator demographics and linguistic context.
proposals.method: We will extend HONEST by designing new, task-specific templates and evaluation protocols. For dialogue, we will create multi-turn prompts where a model must continue a conversation given a persona and a history containing protected attribute cues. For summarization, we will develop source texts with balanced gender/group mentions and analyze generated summaries for representational distortion. We will employ a social systems approach, analyzing bias across multiple languages and model architectures (e.g., T5, GPT-2), and incorporate annotator studies to assess the perceived harm of the generated outputs, linking quantitative HONEST scores to human judgment.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic toolkit for measuring social bias in dialogue and summarization using HONEST principles, enabling more comprehensive model auditing. It will provide empirical evidence on how generative task design influences bias manifestation, offering actionable insights for developers of safer language technologies. The study will also contribute to methodological discussions on cross-task evaluation fairness.
proposals.novelty: While bias in generation is known, adapting the structured, lexicon-based HONEST approach beyond sentence completion to interactive and compressive tasks is novel. Furthermore, integrating the temporal and second-language stability analysis of annotator agreement (from Hovy's prior work) into the validation of these new metrics represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: AUDIT: Augmenting User-Driven Interpretation Tools for Transparent and Controllable Language Technologies
proposals.motivation: Current NLP systems are predominantly extractive, optimizing for model performance at the expense of user understanding and control. This creates documented issues of bias, opacity, and disempowerment, particularly for marginalized groups, as seen in gender performance gaps in speech recognition and instability in hate speech labeling. There is a critical need to develop a complementary paradigm of NLP tools explicitly designed to augment user agency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing NLP systems that provide real-time, interpretable feedback on model behavior and uncertainty, users can achieve greater comprehension and exert more effective control over system outputs, leading to more equitable and trustworthy interactions.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular framework that integrates with existing models (e.g., ASR or text classifiers). It will employ perturbation-based explanation methods (e.g., LIME) and confidence calibration techniques to generate user-facing signals about which input features most influence a prediction and its reliability. We will conduct controlled user studies, adapting methodologies from Hovy's work on annotator agreement, to evaluate how different feedback modalities (e.g., visual highlighting, uncertainty scores) affect user correction rates and trust across demographic groups and language contexts.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a concrete technical framework and evaluation methodology for agency-enhancing NLP. It will provide empirical evidence on how explainable AI (XAI) techniques can be operationalized for user control, moving beyond developer-centric diagnostics, and offer new insights into the intersection of social factors and human-AI interaction.
proposals.novelty: While XAI is an active field, this proposal uniquely frames it as building the 'dual' of extractive systems, explicitly prioritizing user augmentation over model extraction. It integrates social awareness—regarding gender, language, and annotator perspective—directly into the technical design of interactive tools, not just as a post-hoc analysis.

proposals.title: Beyond the English Anchor: A Systematic Evaluation of Non-English Source Languages for Zero-Shot Cross-Lingual Transfer
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in multilingual NLP (pre-2026) assumes English as the universal source language for zero-shot transfer, reinforcing its linguistic hegemony and potentially biasing model performance. This assumption remains largely untested, ignoring the structural and resource-related properties of other potential source languages. Investigating non-English sources is crucial for developing more equitable and efficient language technologies, aligning with calls for socially aware NLP.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effectiveness of a source language for zero-shot transfer is not inherent to English but is a function of its typological proximity to target languages and the quality/quantity of its pre-training data. Therefore, strategically chosen non-English source languages can match or surpass English-based transfer performance for specific language families.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled experiment using a pre-trained multilingual model like XLM-R or mT5. We will fine-tune separate model instances on identical, high-resource tasks (e.g., named entity recognition, sentiment analysis) using different source languages (e.g., German, Russian, Mandarin, Swahili). We will then evaluate zero-shot performance on a diverse set of target languages, controlling for data size and domain. Analysis will correlate performance gaps with linguistic distance metrics and pre-training corpus statistics, extending Hovy's prior work on annotator stability and gender gaps to systemic language bias.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evidence challenging the necessity of English as a transfer source, offering a data-driven framework for selecting optimal source languages. It will contribute to more equitable multilingual NLP by reducing dependency on English-centric pipelines and inform more efficient resource allocation for low-resource language support.
proposals.novelty: While prior work explores cross-lingual transfer, it almost exclusively uses English as the source; systematically benchmarking non-English sources for zero-shot performance is a novel and critical gap. This study uniquely combines typological analysis with empirical model evaluation to deconstruct a foundational but unexamined assumption in the field.

proposals.title: Beyond Parallel Corpora: Modeling Cross-Lingual Topic Dynamics in Non-Parallel Social Media Discourse
proposals.motivation: Multilingual NLP relies heavily on parallel corpora, but real-world discourse across languages is rarely perfectly aligned. Social media discussions on topics like migration or climate policy evolve independently in different linguistic communities, reflecting cultural and social biases. Existing cross-lingual models fail to capture these nuanced, non-parallel topic structures, limiting their applicability to socially aware language technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained to align latent topics from non-parallel, temporally aligned document streams in multiple languages will reveal systematic cross-lingual discourse patterns and performance gaps linked to social variables like annotator demographics and gender-marked language.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend multilingual topic models (e.g., Polylingual Topic Models) and cross-lingual contextual embeddings (e.g., mBERT, XLM-R) to relax the parallel document assumption. Using temporally aligned social media corpora (e.g., Twitter/X data on shared global events), we will train a model with a shared topic space across languages but language-specific topic-word distributions. We will incorporate social metadata (e.g., inferred user gender, annotator labels from prior hate speech studies) as covariates to analyze their influence on topic prevalence and alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for analyzing cross-lingual discourse without parallel data, directly applicable to monitoring hate speech or misinformation across languages. It will contribute new insights into how social factors manifest in non-parallel content, extending research on performance gaps in multilingual systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly modeling the *relationship* between non-identical, socially-grounded content across languages, moving beyond word- or document-level alignment to discover latent thematic bridges, informed by Hovy's prior work on annotator stability and gender performance gaps.

proposals.title: Beyond Fixed K: A Cross-Linguistic Framework for Variable-Granularity Topic Modeling
proposals.motivation: Current multilingual topic models assume a uniform number of topics (K) across languages, forcing all corpora into the same conceptual granularity. This ignores linguistic and cultural realities where discourse structures, lexical specificity, and conceptual taxonomies differ. For tasks like hate speech detection or social science analysis, this rigidity can obscure meaningful, language-specific thematic distinctions, leading to biased or impoverished representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that allowing the number and specificity of topics to vary across languages in a joint model will yield more interpretable, culturally grounded topics and improve downstream task performance on language-specific social phenomena compared to fixed-K baselines.
proposals.method: We will extend neural topic models like the Embedded Topic Model (ETM) or a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) framework. Instead of a single shared topic matrix, we will implement a hierarchical prior over topics, allowing languages to share a global set of coarse concepts while also instantiating language-specific fine-grained subtopics. Model training will use a multilingual corpus (e.g., Europarl, Twitter data) with document-level alignments or comparable collections. Inference will determine the effective number of active topics per language.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, scalable method for cross-linguistic topic modeling that respects inter-language variation. It will contribute a diagnostic framework for analyzing where and why topic granularity diverges, offering insights for multilingual NLP system design. The resulting models will be more suitable for socially aware language technologies analyzing phenomena like hate speech, which manifest differently across linguistic communities.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored asymmetric topic modeling and hierarchical structures, the explicit decoupling of topic granularity from a fixed K across languages in a single, jointly trained neural model is novel. It directly challenges the standard multilingual topic modeling paradigm by incorporating linguistic relativity into the model's core architecture.

proposals.title: Diachronic Cross-Lingual Topic Dynamics: Modeling Temporal Shifts and Social Bias Propagation
proposals.motivation: Current multilingual topic models are largely static, failing to capture how discourse evolves differently across languages and cultures over time. This is a critical gap for socially aware NLP, as understanding these dynamics is essential to track the spread of harmful ideologies, misinformation, or evolving social attitudes. Pre-2026, there is a need to move beyond monolingual diachronic analysis to a truly comparative, multilingual framework that accounts for temporal and cultural variance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that topics evolve at different rates and trajectories across languages, and that these divergences are systematically linked to socio-demographic variables (e.g., annotator demographics, platform-specific norms) and can reveal latent propagation pathways of socially relevant concepts like hate or gendered discourse.
proposals.method: We will extend Dynamic Topic Models (DTM) and the multilingual BERTopic framework to a cross-lingual, diachronic setting. Using aligned multilingual document streams (e.g., news or social media corpora in English, German, Italian, Spanish) over a 5-10 year span, we will train a model that jointly learns temporally shifting topic distributions and their cross-lingual alignments via shared embedding spaces (e.g., LaBSE). We will incorporate annotator agreement and stability metrics from prior hate speech work to weight data and analyze how labeling subjectivity correlates with topic volatility.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified framework for quantifying and visualizing topic evolution across languages, offering new insights into cultural and temporal bias in language technologies. It will deliver a diagnostic tool for identifying languages or time periods where model performance (e.g., in hate speech detection or ASR) may degrade due to unmodeled topic shifts, directly addressing the gender and hate performance gaps observed in Hovy's prior research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of diachronic modeling with cross-lingual alignment and the explicit incorporation of social variables (annotator stability, demographic gaps) to explain topic dynamics, moving beyond purely lexical or statistical drift.

proposals.title: Genre Gaps and Social Biases: A Systematic Evaluation of NLP Models Across Literary, Academic, and Instructional Texts
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 NLP models are predominantly trained and evaluated on a narrow set of genres like news, reviews, and social media, which may not reflect their performance in other socially and linguistically important domains. This creates a critical gap in understanding how model biases, particularly concerning gender and hate speech, manifest in genres such as literature, academic writing, or instructional manuals, where language use, power dynamics, and social meaning differ substantially.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that state-of-the-art models for tasks like sentiment analysis, toxicity detection, and named entity recognition will exhibit significant performance disparities and amplified social biases (e.g., gender, hate) when applied to under-represented text genres, due to domain shift and genre-specific linguistic conventions.
proposals.method: We will select 2-3 pre-trained models (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa) fine-tuned on standard benchmarks for sentiment, hate speech, and gender bias detection. We will systematically evaluate them on a newly curated multi-genre corpus spanning literary fiction, scientific abstracts, and legal/instructional texts, annotated for target tasks and demographic references. Following Hovy's prior work on annotator agreement and multilingual gaps, we will employ error analysis and fairness metrics (e.g., equalized odds, performance gaps across demographic mentions) to quantify genre-specific failure modes and bias amplification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive audit of genre-based performance gaps in socially relevant NLP tasks, directly extending research on model fairness beyond web-based data. It will yield actionable insights for creating more robust, genre-aware evaluation frameworks and highlight the need for more diverse training data to mitigate socially harmful model behaviors.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined bias in common web genres, a systematic, multi-task investigation into how both performance and social biases shift across high-stakes, under-studied genres like literature and academic text is novel. It uniquely combines Hovy's expertise in social bias, annotator stability, and multilingual evaluation within a new dimension of genre diversity.

proposals.title: Adversarial Topic Alignment for Mitigating Cross-Lingual Bias in Hate Speech Detection
proposals.motivation: Multilingual NLP models often suffer from performance disparities across languages, partly due to topic distribution shifts between source and target language corpora. This is critical for socially impactful tasks like hate speech detection, where topic misalignment can amplify demographic biases and degrade model fairness, as shown in prior work on gender performance gaps and annotator instability. Addressing this misalignment is a key step towards socially aware language technologies that perform equitably across languages.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying adversarial domain adaptation to align latent topic distributions between language domains will reduce cross-lingual performance gaps and mitigate topic-induced biases in downstream classification tasks like hate speech detection.
proposals.method: We will extend a multilingual BERT-based hate speech classifier with a Gradient Reversal Layer (GRL) adversarial component. The primary classifier is trained to predict hate speech, while the adversarial discriminator is trained to predict the source language domain from the model's hidden representations. The GRL forces the encoder to learn language-invariant features, effectively aligning topic distributions. We will evaluate on existing multilingual hate speech datasets, comparing performance gaps and fairness metrics (e.g., demographic parity) against a non-adapted baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete method for reducing topic bias in cross-lingual transfer, directly contributing to the development of more equitable multilingual systems. It will offer new empirical insights into the relationship between topic distribution, language domain, and social bias, advancing the practice of socially aware NLP.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial domain adaptation is established for general domain shift, its targeted application to align *topic* distributions for *social bias mitigation* in multilingual hate speech detection is novel. This integrates Hovy's expertise in social bias, multilingual systems, and annotator analysis with adversarial training in a new, problem-specific way.

proposals.title: Distilling Social Biases: Knowledge Distillation for Efficient and Fair Cross-Lingual Topic Models
proposals.motivation: Large multilingual models like mBERT or XLM-R are powerful but computationally expensive and known to encode social biases, including gender and racial stereotypes, which can propagate to downstream applications. There is a pressing need for efficient, deployable models that retain cross-lingual knowledge while mitigating these harmful biases, especially for resource-constrained environments and sensitive tasks like hate speech monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully designed knowledge distillation process, using a large multilingual teacher and a smaller student architecture, can produce a compressed cross-lingual topic model that not only preserves task performance but also exhibits reduced demographic bias compared to its teacher.
proposals.method: We will use a large pre-trained multilingual model (e.g., XLM-R) as a teacher to generate soft topic distributions over a multilingual corpus. We will distill this knowledge into a smaller, more efficient student model based on a cross-lingual topic architecture (e.g., a neural variational inference model). Crucially, we will incorporate a bias-aware distillation loss, inspired by pre-2026 fairness constraints, that penalizes the student for replicating known gender and racial bias patterns identified in the teacher's representations on controlled diagnostic sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodology for bias-aware model compression in multilingual NLP. It will yield a publicly available, efficient cross-lingual topic model and provide empirical analysis on the transfer of social biases during distillation, a relatively unexplored area in model efficiency research.
proposals.novelty: While knowledge distillation for model compression is established, its application to cross-lingual topic modeling with an explicit objective to reduce, rather than just replicate, the teacher's social biases is novel. This bridges Dirk Hovy's expertise in social NLP and multilingual systems with efficient model design.

proposals.title: Topic-Language Decoupling: Probing Cultural and Structural Biases in Multilingual Topic Models
proposals.motivation: Multilingual topic models are widely used for cross-lingual content analysis, but they assume topics are language-agnostic. This assumption may obscure systematic biases where topic expression is confounded by language-specific cultural norms or structural features. Understanding this link is crucial for developing socially aware NLP systems that do not inadvertently encode linguistic stereotypes in their representations of content.
proposals.hypothesis: The topic distributions generated by standard multilingual models retain sufficient language-specific signal to allow accurate prediction of a document's original language, indicating a failure to fully decouple content from linguistic form.
proposals.method: We will train a classifier (e.g., a logistic regression or a simple feed-forward network) to predict the language of a document using only its inferred topic distribution vector from a pre-trained multilingual topic model (e.g., M3L or a ZeroShotTM variant). We will conduct this analysis on a large-scale, topic-diverse multilingual corpus (e.g., Wikipedia or Europarl), controlling for domain. The study will systematically vary the number of topics and the model's training regime to identify which modeling choices increase or decrease language predictability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel diagnostic for bias in multilingual topic models, quantifying the entanglement of language and content. It will offer practical guidance for model development and auditing, advancing the goal of creating more equitable language technologies. The findings will also contribute to methodological discussions on annotator bias and stability in cross-lingual labeling tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines performance gaps across languages, we invert the standard pipeline to treat language as the prediction target from topic features. This directly tests the core assumption of language-agnosticism in multilingual topic modeling, a perspective not explored in existing literature.

proposals.title: Topic-Aware Neural Machine Translation: Mitigating Gender and Domain Bias via Cross-Lingual Topic Features
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation (NMT) systems often produce biased or inaccurate translations, particularly for gender-marked language and low-resource domains, due to a lack of contextual and pragmatic understanding. Pre-2026 research in socially aware NLP highlights that performance gaps, such as those related to gender, are systemic. This project addresses the need for NMT models to incorporate deeper, discourse-level information to improve translation fidelity and reduce social biases.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling cross-lingual document-level topics and integrating them as conditioning features into an NMT architecture will improve translation accuracy, particularly for gender agreement and domain-specific terminology, by providing crucial contextual constraints.
proposals.method: We will first train a multilingual topic model (e.g., a neural variant of Polylingual Topic Models) on a large, aligned document corpus (e.g., Europarl, TED talks) to infer cross-lingual topic distributions. These topic vectors will be integrated as an additional conditioning signal into a state-of-the-art Transformer-based NMT model (e.g., a modified Marian or Fairseq architecture) via topic-aware attention or concatenation with the encoder output. We will evaluate on controlled test sets designed to probe gender bias (following Hovy et al.'s methodology on speech recognition gaps) and domain adaptation, using automatic metrics (BLEU, chrF) and targeted error analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, interpretable method for injecting document-level context into NMT, potentially reducing contextual ambiguity. It contributes to the field of socially aware language technologies by offering a technical pathway to mitigate performance disparities linked to gender and domain, advancing beyond surface-level fixes.
proposals.novelty: While topic models have been used for MT in pre-neural eras, their integration as explicit, trainable features within modern, end-to-end NMT systems to address social biases like gender is unexplored. This bridges Hovy's work on performance gaps and annotator stability with core MT methodology.

proposals.title: Inferring Demographics from Text: A Socially-Aware, Multi-Annotator Framework for Robust and Explainable Predictions
proposals.motivation: Demographic inference from text is crucial for auditing NLP systems for fairness and bias, as shown by pre-2026 work on gender performance gaps in speech recognition. However, most existing models rely on large, explicitly labeled datasets, which are scarce, privacy-invasive, and often fail to account for the fluidity and intersectionality of social categories. This limits their applicability in real-world, multilingual contexts where demographic labels are unavailable.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained on a multi-annotator, disagreement-aware framework will produce more robust, stable, and socially-grounded demographic inferences than models trained on aggregated 'gold' labels, by explicitly capturing the inherent subjectivity and context-dependence of demographic perception.
proposals.method: We will adapt a multi-task transformer architecture (e.g., based on BERT or XLM-R) to predict demographic attributes (gender, age, language background) from social media text. Inspired by recent work on annotator disagreement in hate speech, we will model each annotator's demographic judgments separately as subtasks, rather than aggregating labels. We will incorporate social and linguistic features (e.g., stylistic, syntactic) as auxiliary inputs and use attention mechanisms to provide explainable rationales for the model's predictions, linking them to specific linguistic markers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel modeling framework for demographic inference that is robust to label noise and subjectivity, 2) new insights into the linguistic correlates of perceived demographics, and 3) a publicly available benchmark dataset with multi-annotator demographic labels for multiple languages, facilitating future research in socially aware NLP.
proposals.novelty: While prior work infers demographics from aggregated labels, our approach uniquely centers annotator disagreement as a core signal, not noise, and integrates explainability to make the link between language features and demographic predictions transparent and auditable.

proposals.title: Beyond Single Axes: Modeling Intersectional Demographic Interactions in Language Use and NLP System Performance
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 NLP fairness research has largely examined demographic factors like gender or age in isolation, despite real-world language use being shaped by their intersection. This single-axis analysis risks overlooking significant performance disparities for specific subgroups, leading to incomplete and potentially harmful fairness assessments. A systematic study of these interaction effects is crucial for developing socially aware language technologies that serve diverse populations equitably.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the combined influence of demographic factors (e.g., gender and age) on language use and model performance is not merely additive but exhibits significant interaction effects, leading to unique error patterns for intersectional subgroups that are not captured by single-factor analyses.
proposals.method: We will leverage large-scale, multi-attribute demographic corpora (e.g., Reddit with user-provided age/gender, or curated speech datasets) to train regression models with interaction terms, quantifying the effect of combined factors on linguistic features. We will then audit performance of pre-trained models (e.g., BERT-family models for text, wav2vec 2.0 for speech) on stratified test sets, using statistical tests (ANOVA with interaction effects) to identify significant subgroup performance gaps. The analysis will be extended to annotator behavior, using mixed-effects models to study stability in hate speech labeling across demographic intersections.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive framework for quantifying intersectional demographic interactions in NLP, moving beyond single-axis fairness audits. It will deliver actionable insights for creating more nuanced bias mitigation strategies and contribute methodological tools for intersectional analysis in model evaluation and dataset annotation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Hovy and others has identified gaps for single factors, this proposal systematically models their statistical interaction, a critical step from describing disparities to explaining their compounded origins. It uniquely bridges the analysis of language use, model performance, and annotator behavior under an intersectional lens.

proposals.title: Adapting to the User: Demographic-Aware Fine-Tuning for NLP via Speaker Adaptation Techniques
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 NLP models often exhibit performance disparities across demographic groups, such as gender and language background, as shown in hate speech labeling and multilingual tasks. These biases can lead to unfair and unreliable systems for users from underrepresented groups. While speaker adaptation in speech recognition has successfully personalized models to acoustic characteristics, this principle remains underexplored for adapting text-based NLP models to user demographics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that speaker adaptation techniques, such as learning speaker-specific i-vector or x-vector embeddings, can be effectively repurposed to learn demographic-aware representations for text, enabling more equitable and personalized model performance across user groups.
proposals.method: We will adapt the i-vector framework, a proven method in speech, to the text domain. For a target task (e.g., hate speech detection), we will train a base model while simultaneously learning a low-dimensional demographic vector (dem-vector) from user metadata (e.g., gender, L1). This dem-vector will condition the model's internal representations via feature-wise linear modulation (FiLM). We will evaluate adaptation efficacy by comparing performance gaps across groups on benchmarks like DynaHate against a non-adaptive baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel technical framework for demographic adaptation in NLP, directly addressing fairness gaps. It will yield a reproducible method for creating more equitable systems and offer new insights into how demographic signals are encoded in textual data, bridging speech and NLP adaptation literatures.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and technical transfer of well-established speaker adaptation paradigms from speech processing to demographic adaptation for text-based NLP models, a cross-modal analogy not yet systematically explored.

proposals.title: Demographic-Aware Language Models at Scale: Mitigating Performance Gaps with Massive Web-Crawled Corpora
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Hovy's own work, has established that NLP models exhibit significant performance disparities across demographic groups, particularly in speech recognition and hate speech detection. These gaps are often attributed to biased and limited training data. Scaling up to massive, naturally occurring datasets with user-provided demographic metadata offers a crucial, yet unexplored, path to understanding and mitigating these systemic biases.
proposals.hypothesis: Training large-scale language and speech models on orders-of-magnitude larger, web-crawled corpora annotated with inferred or self-reported user demographics (e.g., gender, language variety, age) will significantly reduce group-specific performance gaps in core NLP tasks compared to models trained on standard, demographically-neutral corpora.
proposals.method: We will construct a massive corpus by scraping publicly available text and speech data from social media platforms (e.g., Twitter, Reddit), leveraging user profile metadata for demographic signals. We will use a multi-task learning framework, building on architectures like BERT or wav2vec 2.0, where the primary task (e.g., ASR, hate speech classification) is jointly optimized with auxiliary demographic prediction tasks. This approach incentivizes the model to learn demographically robust representations. Evaluation will rigorously benchmark performance gaps across groups using stratified testing sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical evidence on the effects of massive demographic-aware pre-training, establishing a new paradigm for bias mitigation. It will yield a publicly released, large-scale demographic corpus and pre-trained models, enabling future research into socially aware language technologies. The findings will directly inform practices for building more equitable large language models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has analyzed biases in existing models or used small, curated demographic datasets, this proposal is novel in its scale and proactive approach: it aims to *construct* massive demographic corpora from the web and use them to *train* foundational models, moving beyond post-hoc analysis to upstream intervention.

proposals.title: Modeling Disagreement as Social Signal: A Multi-Annotator, Multi-Task Framework for Subjective NLP
proposals.motivation: Many critical NLP tasks, such as hate speech detection, toxicity classification, and sentiment analysis, are inherently subjective, leading to systematic and meaningful disagreement among annotators. Current models often treat this disagreement as noise to be eliminated, forcing a single 'gold standard' label and ignoring the rich social and contextual signals embedded in divergent perspectives. This limits model robustness, fairness, and interpretability, especially for socially impactful applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling annotator disagreement explicitly as a multi-dimensional signal—reflecting factors like annotator demographics, linguistic background, and task framing—will yield models that are more robust, generalizable, and transparent for subjective classification tasks than models trained to predict aggregated labels.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-task learning framework where the primary task is to predict the distribution of annotations per instance, not a single label. We will use a transformer-based architecture (e.g., BERT, XLM-R) with task-specific heads. One head predicts the label distribution, while auxiliary heads are trained to predict annotator attributes (e.g., inferred gender, L1 language) and item difficulty, using pre-2026 datasets with annotator metadata (e.g., DynaHate, MultiTox). We will employ uncertainty quantification techniques like Monte Carlo Dropout to model predictive uncertainty, explicitly separating epistemic (model) uncertainty from aleatoric (data/annotator) uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, practical framework for building NLP systems that embrace, rather than suppress, subjectivity. It will contribute new evaluation metrics for model performance on subjective tasks beyond accuracy, and offer insights into how social factors systematically shape language interpretation, advancing the field of socially aware language technologies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has modeled disagreement via distributional labels, our approach uniquely integrates the prediction of annotator social attributes and item difficulty as auxiliary tasks within a single model, explicitly linking the structure of disagreement to measurable annotator and item characteristics to improve learning and interpretation.

proposals.title: Predicting Annotator Consensus: A Model-Based Approach to Quantifying Label Certainty in Subjective NLP Tasks
proposals.motivation: Annotation disagreement is a core challenge in subjective NLP tasks like hate speech detection, reflecting genuine ambiguity and divergent social perspectives. Current models typically predict a single label, discarding valuable information about where annotators agree or disagree. A method to predict consensus can improve dataset quality assessment, inform model confidence, and highlight systematically ambiguous instances, which is crucial for socially aware language technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that linguistic and contextual features of a data instance, combined with annotator demographic and task-specific metadata, can be used to reliably predict the level of annotator agreement, independent of the final label. This consensus signal is a distinct, learnable property of the annotation process.
proposals.method: We will train a multi-task neural architecture (e.g., a transformer encoder with task-specific heads) on existing datasets with multiple annotations per item (e.g., hate speech, sentiment, toxicity).
