The primary task will be to predict the observed inter-annotator agreement score (e.g., Fleiss' kappa or Krippendorff's alpha computed per instance). A secondary auxiliary task will predict the majority label. We will incorporate features from Hovy's prior work on annotator demographics and temporal influence as model inputs or through adversarial debiasing layers to analyze their effect on consensus prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for modeling annotation consensus as a first-class prediction target. It will yield a tool for pre-screening data to flag ambiguous items before large-scale annotation and offer new insights into the linguistic correlates of subjective interpretation, particularly for socially charged content. The model's consensus predictions can serve as a confidence measure for downstream classifiers.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes disagreement post-hoc, we propose to directly and proactively predict consensus likelihood. This shifts the focus from learning a 'gold' label to learning the meta-property of label stability, integrating social and annotator-centric factors into the prediction model itself.

proposals.title: Modeling Implicit Personality and Social Bias in Multilingual Language Models: A Representation Learning Approach
proposals.motivation: Pre-trained language models (LMs) are known to encode and amplify social biases, including gender stereotypes, which can perpetuate harm in downstream applications. However, a systematic, multilingual analysis of how these models represent broader personality constructs—linked to demographic attributes and hate speech perception—remains underexplored. This gap limits our ability to build socially aware language technologies that can account for the nuanced relationship between language, identity, and harmful content.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multilingual contextual language models (e.g., mBERT, XLM-R) encode latent representations of personality traits and social group associations, and that these representations systematically vary across languages and correlate with annotator disagreement patterns in subjective tasks like hate speech labeling.
proposals.method: We will employ a representation probing framework. First, we extract contextual embeddings from models like mBERT and XLM-R for sentences annotated with demographic and personality survey data (e.g., from social media). Using linear and non-linear probing classifiers, we quantify how well personality and social group membership can be decoded from these embeddings. Second, we will correlate the geometric distances in these learned representation spaces with intra-annotator disagreement metrics from hate speech datasets, adapting methods from Hovy's prior work on annotation stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven mapping between neural representations and psychosocial constructs, offering a more granular understanding of bias in LMs beyond binary attributes. It will contribute a methodological framework for diagnosing the social dimensions of model representations and their consequences for annotation tasks, directly informing the development of more equitable multilingual NLP systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on isolated demographic biases, this proposal uniquely links the neural representation of continuous personality spectra to practical annotation instability across languages, integrating psycholinguistic constructs with representation learning analysis.

proposals.title: Personality as a Social Signal: A Multi-Annotator Framework for Robust Fake News and Hate Speech Detection
proposals.motivation: Current NLP models for socially critical tasks like fake news and hate speech detection often treat language as a monolithic signal, ignoring the profound influence of the author's psychological profile on linguistic expression. This leads to performance gaps across demographic groups and unstable annotations, as evidenced by pre-2026 work on gender gaps in speech recognition and intra-annotator disagreement. Incorporating a structured model of author personality can provide a crucial social and psychological context to improve model robustness and fairness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling author personality traits, inferred from linguistic style, will improve the accuracy and generalizability of classifiers for fake news detection and hate speech identification, particularly for ambiguous or adversarial examples where surface-level cues are insufficient.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-task learning framework where a primary task model (e.g., a BERT-based classifier for fake news) is augmented with a personality prediction auxiliary task. Personality will be operationalized using the Big Five Inventory (BFI) dimensions, with training labels derived from existing stylometric datasets (e.g., essays tagged with BFI scores) or via distant supervision from social media profiles. The model will use a shared encoder with task-specific heads, and we will experiment with both hard-parameter sharing and adversarial debiasing techniques to isolate the beneficial contextual signal of personality from potential confounding biases.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, socially-informed architecture that integrates psycholinguistic features into standard NLP pipelines. It will provide empirical evidence on the utility of personality for contentious classification tasks and release a new dataset pairing news articles or social media posts with inferred author personality vectors, facilitating further research in socially aware language technologies.
proposals.novelty: While personality prediction is a known NLP task, its systematic use as a feature to *improve* other high-stakes social NLP tasks is underexplored. This proposal uniquely bridges Dirk Hovy's expertise in social bias, annotator stability, and multilingual systems by treating personality as a fundamental, stabilizing variable in human language production.

proposals.title: Modeling Normative Constructs: A Contextual Inference Framework from Prescriptive Demographic Annotations
proposals.motivation: NLP systems increasingly rely on datasets where annotators assign demographic labels (e.g., perceived gender, race) based on social cues, embedding human normative judgments into training data. This process, as shown in pre-2026 work on annotator disagreement and bias, risks reifying stereotypes and encoding context-dependent social constructs as fixed attributes. Understanding the specific contextual triggers of these labeling norms is crucial for developing socially aware language technologies that can critically engage with, rather than blindly propagate, these embedded belief systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The inconsistencies in prescriptive demographic labeling across different linguistic and situational contexts are not noise, but systematic outputs of an underlying, conditional normative system that can be reverse-engineered to map the contextual boundaries and triggers of demographic category construction.
proposals.method: We will collect or utilize existing datasets with prescriptive demographic labels (e.g., perceived gender from speech or text) and rich contextual metadata (e.g., topic, dialect, pragmatic function). Building on Hovy's work on annotator modeling, we will treat each labeling instance as a data point generated by a latent normative system. We will employ a multi-task or mixture-of-experts model architecture, where a context encoder (e.g., based on BERT or similar pre-2026 transformers) predicts the assigned label and, crucially, also infers the likely contextual rule or 'norm cluster' activated for that decision. This allows us to cluster contexts where specific labeling norms apply and identify their linguistic correlates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, data-driven framework for modeling how social categories are situationally constructed in annotation tasks, moving beyond measuring bias to explaining its conditional logic. It will offer new methodologies for auditing training datasets and for designing annotation guidelines that account for context-dependence, contributing directly to the fields of fair ML and socially aware NLP.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies demographic performance gaps or annotator disagreement, this proposal explicitly models the *generative process* of prescriptive labeling as a context-dependent system to be inferred, treating demographic attributes as fluid outputs rather than static inputs to the annotation task.

proposals.title: Adversarial Causal Disentanglement for Stereotype-Invariant Mental State Inference from Language
proposals.motivation: Language technologies for inferring mental states (e.g., emotion, intent) from text are increasingly used in social and clinical contexts, but they risk perpetuating harmful demographic stereotypes by learning spurious correlations from biased training data. This is a critical issue in socially aware NLP, as identified in Hovy's prior work on gender performance gaps and annotator disagreement in hate speech labeling. Developing methods that study internal states without relying on biased social categories is essential for equitable and robust language systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a causal graph formalizing the relationship between mental states, language, and demographic attributes, combined with an adversarial training objective, can produce mental state representations that are invariant to demographic information, thereby preventing stereotype-based inference.
proposals.method: We will construct a causal graph with mental state as a latent confounder of language and demographic attributes. Using a VAE or similar architecture, we will learn to encode text into separate mental state and demographic subspaces. An adversarial discriminator, trained to predict demographic attributes from the mental state subspace, will provide a gradient reversal penalty to enforce invariance. Robustness will be evaluated on counterfactually generated text using pre-2026 methods like controlled perturbation or generative models (e.g., GPT-2/3) to create non-stereotypical pairings of mental state language and demographic cues.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, causality-informed framework for debiasing mental state inference, moving beyond correlation-based fairness approaches. It will provide a new evaluation paradigm using counterfactual text generation to test for stereotypical reasoning. The resulting models will enable more reliable and equitable study of language about internal states across different demographic groups.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial debiasing exists, its integration with a formal causal model for mental state language is novel. Furthermore, applying counterfactual evaluation specifically to challenge stereotypical patterns in mental state attribution represents a significant methodological advance over standard bias benchmarks.

proposals.title: Ecological Inference for Demographic Prediction: Inverting the Causal Lens on Mental State Language
proposals.motivation: Psycholinguistics and NLP often treat demographic attributes like gender or age as predictors of linguistic patterns, including mental state expression, risking ecological fallacies and reinforcing deterministic assumptions. This unidirectional modeling obscures the probabilistic, context-dependent nature of the relationship. Adapting the ecological inference framework from epidemiology offers a rigorous, statistically sound method to challenge this causal directionality and quantify the strength of demographic signals within linguistic ecologies.
proposals.hypothesis: Demographic attributes can be probabilistically estimated from the aggregate linguistic features of mental state descriptions, and the configuration of this reverse prediction reveals which demographic constellations are most strongly linked to specific linguistic ecologies, challenging the assumed primacy of demographics as predictors.
proposals.method: We will collect large-scale textual datasets (e.g., from mental health forums, diary studies) where mental state language is prevalent and aggregate demographic distributions (e.g., gender ratios, age brackets) are known but individual-level links are withheld. Treating each document collection (e.g., a forum thread) as an 'area,' we will extract linguistic features (lexical, syntactic, semantic) related to mental states. We will adapt a multilevel regression and post-stratification (MRP) model, where the 'area-level' linguistic features predict latent individual-level demographic category probabilities, which are then aggregated and compared to the known population totals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological bridge between epidemiological statistics and computational sociolinguistics, offering a tool to rigorously test causal assumptions in language analysis. It will yield quantifiable insights into the conditional probabilities linking language and demographics, moving beyond correlation to inform more nuanced, socially aware language technologies that avoid reductive stereotypes.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the inversion of the typical prediction task and the direct adaptation of the ecological inference solution to a linguistic problem, a method not previously applied in this domain. This explicitly tests the reverse causal pathway from language to demographics, a non-obvious direction given the field's standard modeling practices.

proposals.title: Platform-Specific Lexical and Pragmatic Biases: Quantifying and Mitigating Cross-Platform Generalization Failures in Hate Speech Detection
proposals.motivation: Current NLP models for social media analysis are typically trained and evaluated on data from single platforms (e.g., Twitter/X), assuming language use and hate speech patterns generalize. Pre-2026 work by Hovy and others shows significant intra-annotator instability and demographic performance gaps, suggesting social context heavily influences language interpretation. This assumption of cross-platform generalizability is untested and risks deploying biased, ineffective systems on diverse platforms like Reddit, TikTok, or Gab.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hate speech detection models exhibit significant performance degradation when applied to social media platforms not represented in their training data, due to systematic lexical, pragmatic, and community-specific biases that constitute distinct socio-linguistic registers.
proposals.method: We will curate parallel, annotator-aligned hate speech datasets from Twitter, Reddit, and 4chan using a consistent annotation protocol, analyzing annotator agreement stability across platforms. Using pre-2026 transformer architectures (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa), we will train platform-specific models and test cross-platform performance. We will employ interpretability methods (e.g., LIME, integrated gradients) and linguistic feature analysis to isolate platform-specific lexical, syntactic, and pragmatic markers causing generalization failure, then design a simple but novel adversarial domain adaptation layer to mitigate these biases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic quantification of cross-platform generalization failure in a critical social NLP task, moving beyond single-platform studies. It will contribute a methodological framework for auditing platform bias and a lightweight adaptation technique, advancing the development of socially aware language technologies that are robust across digital ecosystems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines demographic or temporal bias, the explicit treatment of social media platforms as distinct socio-linguistic domains requiring adaptation is novel. The approach uniquely combines Hovy's focus on annotator perspective and stability with a systematic cross-platform experimental design.

proposals.title: Intersectional Instability: Decomposing Annotator Subjectivity Across Tasks and Contexts
proposals.motivation: Current NLP models treat annotator demographics as static features to improve prediction, implicitly assuming stable, separable subjective beliefs. However, pre-2026 work on hate speech labeling and speech recognition shows performance gaps and intra-annotator disagreement are complex and influenced by multiple factors. This project addresses the core, untested assumption of annotator stability, which is critical for building socially aware language technologies that genuinely understand the origins of bias and disagreement in data.
proposals.hypothesis: Annotator subjectivity is not a stable, separable trait but is intersectionally constructed, leading to measurable instability in annotation behavior across different tasks and linguistic contexts. This instability can be systematically traced to interactions between demographic attributes and specific data domains.
proposals.method: We will collect annotations for multiple subjective tasks (e.g., hate speech, politeness, formality) from a demographically profiled pool, capturing a rich vector (gender, cultural background, SES, education). Using multi-task learning architectures with shared and task-specific layers, we will model each annotator's decisions. We will then apply techniques like Integrated Gradients or attention analysis to the model to decompose the contribution of each demographic factor per task and context, testing for statistical interactions and shifts in influence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for modeling the fluidity of human judgment, moving beyond demographics as mere inputs. It will offer empirical evidence on when and why annotator beliefs appear stable or fluid, directly informing the design of more nuanced annotation protocols and evaluation metrics for subjective NLP tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses demographics for prediction or analyzes aggregate gaps, this proposal uniquely frames them as a lens to *decompose* and *trace* the origins of subjectivity itself, explicitly testing its stability—a core, unexamined assumption in annotation studies.

proposals.title: A Multi-Task Learning Benchmark: Comparing Deep MTL Architectures for Socially Aware NLP Tasks
proposals.motivation: Multi-task learning (MTL) is a promising paradigm for socially aware NLP, as it can leverage related tasks like hate speech detection, demographic attribute prediction, and sentiment analysis to improve robustness and fairness. However, the field lacks systematic comparisons of deep MTL frameworks against other advanced neural architectures, such as single-task transformers, adapter-based models, and modular networks. A rigorous, empirical benchmark is needed to guide researchers and practitioners in choosing architectures that balance performance, fairness, and computational efficiency for sensitive applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully designed deep MTL framework will outperform strong single-task and adapter-based baselines on a suite of related social NLP tasks, not only in aggregate performance but also in mitigating performance disparities across demographic groups (e.g., gender, language variety) as identified in prior speech and text analysis.
proposals.method: We will construct a benchmark dataset combining existing pre-2026 resources for hate speech, gender bias, and language variety detection. We will implement and rigorously compare: 1) a proposed deep MTL model with hard parameter sharing and task-specific heads; 2) single-task Transformer fine-tuning (e.g., BERT, XLM-R); 3) adapter-based fine-tuning for multi-task learning; and 4) a modular mixture-of-experts architecture. Evaluation will use standard performance metrics (F1, accuracy) and fairness metrics (performance gap analysis across groups) for each task, with statistical significance testing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark comparing neural architectures for multi-task social NLP, offering clear guidance on trade-offs between performance, fairness, and efficiency. It will extend Dirk Hovy's research on annotator stability and performance gaps by explicitly testing whether MTL architectures can reduce such disparities, contributing a practical framework for more equitable language technologies.
proposals.novelty: While MTL is widely used, a direct, fairness-focused comparison against contemporary adapter and modular architectures for sensitive social NLP tasks is novel. The integration of performance gap analysis as a core evaluation criterion moves beyond aggregate metrics to assess architectural impact on equitable outcomes.

proposals.title: Transferring Clinical NLP Architectures for Socially Aware Analysis of Low-Resource and Stigmatized Language
proposals.motivation: Clinical NLP models, such as those trained on medical notes (e.g., BioBERT, ClinicalBERT), are adept at handling sensitive, informal, and ambiguous language under strict privacy and ethical constraints. Social media analysis for tasks like hate speech detection faces similar challenges: informal text, implicit meaning, and sensitivity to demographic factors like gender and language background. However, social media models often lack the robust architectures developed for clinical domains, which are designed for high-stakes, low-resource scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that transformer architectures pre-trained and fine-tuned on clinical notes can be effectively adapted via transfer learning to social media hate speech and gender bias detection tasks, outperforming generic language models, particularly for linguistically complex or low-resource language data.
proposals.method: We will adapt models like ClinicalBERT and BioMed-RoBERTa, which are pre-trained on de-identified clinical text. Using a multi-stage transfer approach, we will first continue pre-training on a large, diverse corpus of social media text (e.g., from Twitter/X and Reddit) to adapt domain vocabulary. We will then fine-tune on existing hate speech and gender bias detection datasets, explicitly analyzing performance gaps across languages and demographic subgroups. The evaluation will include controlled experiments comparing these adapted models against base BERT/RoBERTa and domain-specific social media models (e.g., BERTweet) on metrics of accuracy and fairness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, robust modeling framework for sensitive social media NLP tasks, potentially reducing annotation needs for low-resource languages. It will contribute a systematic analysis of how clinical domain adaptations affect model performance on social variables like gender and language, advancing the field of socially aware language technologies.
proposals.novelty: While cross-domain transfer is common, leveraging the specific architectural and training strengths of clinical NLP—designed for privacy, ambiguity, and low-data settings—for social media hate speech analysis is a novel and non-obvious direction. It directly connects Hovy's work on annotator stability and demographic gaps with advanced transfer learning techniques.

proposals.title: Multimodal Multi-Task Learning for Socially Aware Content Moderation on Social Media
proposals.motivation: Social media content is inherently multimodal, combining text, images, and network context. Pre-2026 hate speech and bias detection systems predominantly analyze text, ignoring crucial visual and relational signals, which limits their effectiveness and can introduce performance gaps across demographic groups. A holistic, multimodal framework is needed to improve the accuracy and fairness of automated content analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: A multimodal MTL framework that jointly learns from text, images, and user network data will outperform text-only models in social computing tasks like hate speech detection and demographic bias assessment, while revealing how performance disparities manifest across modalities.
proposals.method: We will extend existing MTL architectures (e.g., hard parameter sharing) to fuse pre-2026 models: a transformer-based text encoder (e.g., BERT), a convolutional neural network (CNN) or Vision Transformer (ViT) for image features, and a graph neural network (GNN) for user interaction networks. Tasks will include hate speech classification, gender/race bias prediction, and a novel auxiliary task of cross-modal consistency prediction. Training will use datasets like Hateful Memes and annotated Twitter/X corpora, with careful analysis of per-task and per-demographic-group performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified, scalable framework for multimodal social media analysis, directly applicable to content moderation. It will produce new insights into how bias propagates across modalities in AI systems and offer a methodology to audit such systems more comprehensively.
proposals.novelty: While multimodal fusion exists, applying an MTL framework specifically to socially aware tasks (hate, bias) with the explicit inclusion of network structure—inspired by Hovy's work on annotator stability and gender gaps—is novel. It moves beyond single-modality or dual-modality approaches prevalent pre-2026.

proposals.title: Neural Feature Fusion for Socially Aware NLP: Automating the Combination of Lexical and Extra-Linguistic Cues
proposals.motivation: Current NLP systems for socially relevant tasks like hate speech detection or gender bias analysis often rely on manually engineered feature combinations, such as character n-grams and metadata like user demographics. This process is suboptimal and may fail to capture complex, task-specific interactions between linguistic form and social context. A method to automatically learn these combinations is needed to build more robust and equitable language technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neural architecture with dedicated fusion modules can learn more effective, task-specific combinations of character-level lexical features and extra-linguistic metadata than manual feature engineering or simple concatenation, leading to improved performance and more interpretable feature interactions in socially aware NLP tasks.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-input neural architecture. Character n-grams will be processed via a CNN or a small transformer encoder. Extra-linguistic features (e.g., annotator demographics, speaker gender, or temporal metadata) will be encoded via dense embeddings. A central fusion module, such as a gated multimodal unit or cross-attention layer, will be trained to combine these representations. We will evaluate on pre-2026 datasets for hate speech labeling and multilingual ASR gender gap analysis, benchmarking against logistic regression with handcrafted features and simple neural baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a generalizable neural framework for fusing linguistic and social features, moving beyond ad-hoc feature engineering. It will yield new insights into which feature interactions are most predictive for tasks like hate speech annotation stability and bias detection, contributing to the development of more socially aware and transparent NLP systems.
proposals.novelty: While neural networks are standard for feature extraction, their explicit application to *optimize the combination* of classic NLP features (n-grams) and social metadata for fairness and hate speech tasks is novel. This bridges Dirk Hovy's focus on social factors with state-of-the-art deep learning fusion techniques from multimodal research.

proposals.title: Linguistic Probes for Latent Social Structure: Reconstructing Implicit Networks from Multi-Party Discourse
proposals.motivation: Social network analysis often relies on explicit interaction data (e.g., follows, mentions), which can miss subtle, implicit, or offline social structures. Language, however, encodes rich social information through stylistic alignment, topic co-reference, and pragmatic cues. Reconstructing hidden social networks from language data alone is crucial for understanding communities where explicit ties are unavailable or for auditing algorithmic systems that may inadvertently encode social biases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that latent social network structures—such as group affiliations, hierarchical roles, and interpersonal influence—can be reliably inferred from the linguistic traces in multi-party discourse, even in the absence of explicit interaction metadata.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend relational inference methods from social NLP. Using a transformer-based encoder (e.g., BERT, XLM-R), we will extract speaker-level linguistic representations from threaded conversations (e.g., parliamentary debates, online forums). We will then train a graph neural network (GNN) to predict edges in a latent social graph, using supervision from known sub-networks or external validation. The model will be probed for cues like lexical entrainment, politeness strategies, and argumentative stance, informed by pre-2026 work on annotator disagreement and multilingual gender gaps.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology for social structure discovery from unstructured text, contributing to computational social science. It will also offer diagnostic tools to audit language technologies for their implicit modeling of social hierarchies, extending Hovy's prior work on socially aware NLP and bias in speech systems.
proposals.novelty: While existing work uses language to predict node attributes *within* a known network, we invert the paradigm: using language as the *sole evidence* to reconstruct the unknown network itself, focusing on implicit ties rather than explicit interactions.

proposals.title: Beyond Consensus: A Large-Scale Analysis of Cross-Lingual and Cross-Cultural Disagreement in Multilingual Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Current multilingual NLP evaluation relies on aggregated gold labels, masking systematic disagreement rooted in cultural and linguistic variation. This obscures model performance for diverse user groups and risks encoding a narrow, often Western-centric, perspective of concepts like hate speech or sentiment. A systematic, large-scale investigation of disagreement is crucial for developing socially aware language technologies that are robust and equitable across languages.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that annotator disagreement in multilingual evaluation datasets is not random noise but exhibits predictable, structured patterns correlated with linguistic features (e.g., language family, morphological complexity) and socio-cultural factors (e.g., Hofstede dimensions, legal frameworks).
proposals.method: We will compile a large-scale meta-dataset of existing multilingual evaluation tasks (e.g., hate speech, sentiment, toxicity) with preserved annotator IDs and labels. We will quantify disagreement using metrics like Krippendorff's alpha and annotator entropy, then model it as a function of language and country-level features using mixed-effects regression. We will further analyze disagreement stability by simulating second-language annotator effects, extending Hovy's prior work on intra-annotator agreement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, quantitative map of disagreement across languages and tasks, moving beyond treating it as noise. It will yield practical guidelines for dataset creation and model reporting (e.g., disagreement-aware confidence scores) and a theoretical framework for understanding cultural subjectivity in NLP benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work notes disagreement, this study systematically scales the analysis across many languages and tasks to uncover macro-level patterns, and explicitly links linguistic typology and cultural metrics to annotation variance, moving from observation to prediction.

proposals.title: Operationalizing Socially Aware NLP: A Framework for Auditing and Mitigating Demographic Bias in Sentiment Analysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that NLP models, including those for sentiment analysis, encode and amplify social biases, leading to performance disparities across demographic groups like gender. However, the field lacks a standardized, task-specific framework to systematically audit these biases and implement targeted mitigations. This gap hinders the development of equitable language technologies in high-stakes applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by operationalizing a general socio-technical audit framework for the specific task of sentiment analysis, we can identify systematic demographic performance gaps and that targeted, task-aware data augmentation can reduce these gaps without degrading overall model performance.
proposals.method: We will adapt the general audit framework from Hovy's prior work to sentiment analysis. First, we will curate or construct evaluation sets with demographic metadata (e.g., gender via pronoun analysis, dialect) for English and at least one other language. We will then audit state-of-the-art sentiment models (e.g., fine-tuned BERT, RoBERTa) to quantify performance gaps. For mitigation, we will implement a task-specific data augmentation strategy, generating synthetic training examples via controlled text perturbation (e.g., using back-translation or template-based generation) to increase demographic diversity in sentiment expression, followed by re-training and re-evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, replicable blueprint for bias auditing and mitigation tailored to sentiment analysis, moving beyond general principles. It will deliver empirical evidence on the efficacy of task-aware interventions and release benchmark datasets annotated with demographic cues to facilitate future research in socially aware NLP.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific operationalization of a high-level social awareness framework for a core NLP task, integrating demographic auditing with a targeted, task-preserving mitigation strategy, rather than applying generic debiasing techniques.

proposals.title: Psycholinguistic Probes for Social Bias: Quantifying Semantic Associations in Multilingual Embeddings via Word Association Tests
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that NLP models encode harmful social biases, often measured via static embedding tests like WEAT. However, these methods rely on predefined attribute and target word lists, which may not capture the full, dynamic spectrum of semantic associations present in language models. This is critical for understanding and mitigating bias in downstream tasks like hate speech detection and machine translation, where implicit associations can perpetuate harm.
proposals.hypothesis: Word association tests, a classic psycholinguistic instrument, will reveal a richer and more nuanced landscape of semantic bias in contextual and static embeddings than standard benchmark tests, and these associations will vary systematically across languages and demographic factors of the training data.
proposals.method: We will adapt established word association norms (e.g., the University of South Florida Free Association Norms) and multilingual equivalents to create probe sets for social categories (e.g., gender, ethnicity). For a given seed word (e.g., 'nurse'), we will compute the cosine similarity between its embedding and candidate associate words (e.g., 'woman', 'caring', 'man', 'doctor') in models like BERT and fastText. We will statistically compare the strength of these learned associations against human baselines and across languages, using methods from Hovy's prior work on annotator stability and multilingual performance gaps to control for confounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, cognitively-grounded diagnostic tool for bias in NLP models, moving beyond checklist evaluations. It will contribute a new dataset of psycholinguistically-validated probe pairs for bias evaluation in multiple languages and offer insights into how cultural and linguistic variation shapes bias in language technology.
proposals.novelty: While bias in embeddings is well-studied, applying the specific paradigm of free word association tests—a core method from cognitive psychology—to quantify semantic bias represents a novel methodological fusion. This approach directly connects model-internal associations to human cognitive associations, offering a more naturalistic bias measure.

proposals.title: A Cross-Lingual, Multi-Task Framework for Auditing Demographic Bias in Language and Speech Technologies
proposals.motivation: Current bias auditing frameworks are largely monolingual or bilingual, failing to capture how biases manifest across the world's linguistic diversity. This is a critical gap, as pre-2026 research shows performance disparities in multilingual NLP and speech recognition are linked to gender and other demographic factors, but systematic, scalable evaluation methods are lacking. Extending bias frameworks to many languages is essential for equitable global deployment of language technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a unified, multi-task framework can systematically reveal that demographic bias patterns in language technologies are not consistent across languages, but are instead modulated by linguistic typology, resource availability, and annotator demographics from the source training data.
proposals.method: We will extend existing bias frameworks (e.g., for gender in coreference or speech recognition) to a set of 30+ typologically diverse languages. The method involves: 1) Curating or generating multi-task evaluation suites (e.g., for hate speech detection, ASR, and machine translation) with controlled demographic perturbations. 2) Leveraging massively multilingual models (e.g., mT5, XLS-R) and task-specific models as testbeds. 3) Implementing a systematic analysis pipeline to quantify performance gaps (like the 'humps and pebbles' from Hovy et al.'s ASR work) and correlate them with linguistic features and data provenance, drawing on social and annotator-centric analysis methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first scalable, empirically grounded framework for auditing a broad spectrum of biases across many languages and tasks. It will produce a public benchmark and diagnostic tool for researchers and practitioners, moving the field beyond English-centric bias analysis. The findings will offer concrete guidance for mitigating bias in the development of future multilingual systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving from isolated, single-language or single-task bias studies to an integrated, typologically-informed cross-lingual audit, explicitly connecting technical performance gaps to the social and annotator variables that underpin training data creation for many languages simultaneously.

ideas.description: Operationalize the general framework for concrete tasks like sentiment analysis.

ideas.title: Predicting Contextual Belief States from Mental State Language
ideas.description: This research inverts the typical predictive direction by using language about mental states (e.g., from survey responses or social media) to predict the annotator's or speaker's latent, context-specific belief configurations, rather than predicting mental states from demographics. It directly challenges the assumption that these influencing beliefs are fixed and monolithic by modeling them as a separable, stable-yet-contextual set of dimensions that can be activated differently across annotation tasks or communicative situations. The synergy enables a causal-like analysis of how specific belief contexts, not broad demographics, systematically bias the interpretation and labeling of psychological language. This provides a fine-grained, mechanistic account of subjectivity in annotation, useful for debiasing models and understanding cultural cognition.

ideas.title: Inverting the Causal Lens: Predicting Demographics from Mental State Language via Ecological Inference
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the ecological inference problem from epidemiology to challenge the assumption that demographics predict mental state language. In public health, ecological inference estimates individual-level disease risk from area-level data, avoiding the ecological fallacy. Analogously, we propose to invert the typical prediction direction in psycholinguistics by not predicting mental state from demographics, but predicting demographic attributes from language about mental state. The adapted method involves collecting large-scale textual data expressing mental states (e.g., from forums, diaries, interviews) with known but withheld demographic aggregates. Using a model akin to multilevel regression with post-stratification, we would treat the linguistic features as the 'area-level' data and the demographic categories as latent individual-level outcomes to be estimated. This tests the strength and configuration of the reverse causal pathway, revealing which demographic constellations are most probabilistically linked to specific linguistic ecologies of mental state description.

ideas.title: Few-Shot Belief Learning for Robust Modeling of Noisy Self-Reported Labels
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how to learn a computational model of an individual's belief system from very few examples of their descriptive or prescriptive judgments, particularly in contexts where training data relies on noisy self-reports (e.g., of mental health symptoms) rather than gold-standard clinical diagnoses. The core innovation is to use few-shot belief learning to capture each annotator's unique mapping from their internal state to a self-reported label, modeling their specific biases and heuristics. This allows a downstream model to be trained not on the raw, unreliable labels, but on a de-biased representation that accounts for the individual belief patterns of the data sources, significantly improving generalization to ground truth. The synergy enables robust learning from inherently subjective data by personalizing noise correction at the annotator level with minimal calibration data.

ideas.title: Causal Disentanglement for Stereotype-Robust Inference from Mental State Language
ideas.description: This research develops a method to analyze language about mental states while actively preventing the inference of demographic stereotypes. It uses a causal graph to formally separate the pathways by which mental state influences language from those where demographic correlates create spurious associations. The system is trained with an adversarial objective to produce mental state representations that are invariant to demographic attributes, and its robustness is evaluated on counterfactually generated text that challenges stereotypical patterns. The goal is to enable the study of language about internal states without perpetuating or relying on biased social categories.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning Belief Principles from Annotator Rationales
ideas.description: This approach learns to model an annotator's belief system from very few examples by first collecting structured rationales that explain each judgment. A meta-learning framework is then trained to distill these rationales into a set of core, reusable belief principles, disentangled from task-specific surface features. The system actively tests its learned model by generating counterfactual scenarios and contrastive queries for the annotator to answer, refining its understanding of the belief boundary. This results in a robust model that can generalize the annotator's beliefs to novel and complex cases far beyond the original few-shot context.

ideas.title: Inverse Belief Generation via Inverse Problems in Medical Imaging
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to inverse problems from medical imaging to the original seed idea of generating text for a given belief. In imaging, one reconstructs a source image from sensor data using a known forward model of the imaging process. Analogously, we define a 'forward model' that simulates prescriptive annotation—a function that maps any input text to a belief system label (the 'measurement'). The core research becomes solving the inverse: for a desired belief label, generate the text that this forward model would consistently map to that label. This can be approached by training a generative model (e.g., a conditional language model) where the conditioning signal is derived from the error between the forward model's prediction for generated text and the target label, using iterative optimization or end-to-end training akin to learned image reconstruction techniques. The 'SCALING_DUAL' dimension manifests in scaling both the complexity of the belief system forward model and the fidelity/volume of the generated text corpus.

ideas.title: Modeling Dynamic Intersectional Subjectivity in Annotation
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to expand the set of modeled demographic attributes (e.g., socioeconomic status, cultural background, education) not merely to improve raw prediction accuracy, but to explicitly test and model the assumption that annotator subjectivity is stable and separable. The core synergy is using this richer demographic vector to investigate how subjective beliefs are, in fact, intersectional, context-sensitive, and potentially unstable across different tasks or data domains. This moves beyond using demographics as fixed inputs for prediction, instead employing them as a framework to decompose and trace the origins of annotator disagreement, revealing when and why beliefs appear stable or fluid.

ideas.title: Inverse: Generate Text for a Belief Community's Probabilistic Annotation
ideas.description: This system generates text that would be probabilistically labeled by a specific belief community, capturing the real-world inconsistency and strategic application of their own prescriptive rules. Instead of a single deterministic mapping, it models a distribution of potential annotations from within the community, conditioned on contextual factors and rhetorical intent. The output is text that reflects the nuanced, often contradictory, ways belief labels are actually applied in discourse, making it more robust and realistic for simulating or analyzing ideological communication.

ideas.title: Inferring Context-Dependent Normative Systems from Prescriptive Demographic Labeling
ideas.description: This research direction analyzes prescriptively labeled datasets (e.g., where demographic attributes like gender or race are assigned by annotators based on norms) to reverse-engineer the underlying belief systems. Crucially, it models the demographic attributes in these labels not as fixed truths but as fluid, context-dependent outputs of the normative system itself. By examining inconsistencies and variations in labeling across different contexts within the data, the method can infer a more nuanced, conditional model of the enforced beliefs, mapping the contextual triggers and boundaries of the labeling norms. This reveals how the belief system actively constructs demographic categories situationally rather than merely recognizing static traits.

ideas.title: Multi-Dimensional Belief System Inference from Prescriptive Labeling
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a method to infer the full, multi-dimensional belief landscape of annotators or labeling guidelines from datasets that have been collapsed into single, prescriptive labels. By modeling the underlying, interacting dimensions of subjectivity (e.g., fairness, harm, intent, cultural context) that were compressed during the prescriptive labeling process, the approach can reconstruct the implicit priorities and decision boundaries of the belief system that was enforced. This allows for auditing and diagnosing labeling protocols, quantifying what subjective information was lost, and simulating how different multi-dimensional belief configurations would alter the final labels. The core novelty is moving from inferring a single belief axis to discovering the entire constrained belief space that rationalizes the observed prescriptive outcomes.

ideas.title: Demographically-Grounded Collective Prescription for Mental State Modeling
ideas.description: This research direction inverts the standard assumption of a fixed, researcher-defined 'prescriptive' mental state by having annotators collectively define it during data annotation. Crucially, these annotators are characterized by a rich, expanded set of demographic attributes (e.g., ethnicity, socioeconomic status, cultural background, education, profession). The resulting dataset and model would learn to predict an individual's mental state while also modeling the landscape of prescriptive beliefs about that state as a function of the annotators' demographic composition. This allows for the analysis of how normative judgments about mental health and emotion are shaped by demographic perspective, ultimately building AI systems that are aware of the plurality and potential bias in societal norms.

ideas.title: Text as Geological Strata: Decomposing Subjective Annotation into Objective Segmentation Layers
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that subjectivity permeates the entire annotation process by drawing an analogy from stratigraphy in geology. In stratigraphy, the rock layers themselves are an objective, physical sequence, while the division into named geological periods (e.g., Jurassic, Cretaceous) is a later, subjective interpretive framework. Adapting this solution, the research would treat a given text as an objective linguistic sequence—akin to geological strata—composed of identifiable units like clauses or propositional statements. The methodology would require annotators to first perform a rule-based, objective segmentation of this textual structure, deliberately separating this task from the subsequent application of subjective labels (e.g., sentiment, topic, intent). This forces a decomposition of the annotation pipeline, clearly isolating where subjectivity is introduced (the labeling of pre-segmented units) and where it is not (the identification of the fundamental textual segments themselves), thereby challenging the focus on annotator subjectivity by showing it resides primarily in the label, not in the foundational parsing of the text.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Annotator Belief Landscapes
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology—Species Distribution Modeling (SDM)—to the problem of modeling annotator belief distributions. In ecology, SDM techniques like MaxEnt infer a species' complete potential geographic range from a limited set of observed presence points by modeling its probability distribution across environmental gradients. Analogously, we can treat each annotation task instance (e.g., a specific text prompt or image) as a point in a feature space defined by dimensions like ambiguity, subject matter, or emotional valence. The annotator's provided label (or confidence) is an observed 'presence' of a specific belief. By applying a similar maximum entropy modeling framework, we can move beyond simple aggregation of discrete labels to probabilistically map the full, continuous 'belief niche' of an annotator or demographic group across the feature landscape of possible inputs. This allows for richer modeling of how beliefs shift with context and for predicting belief distributions on new, unseen items, thereby improving the fidelity of crowd-sourced data used to train AI systems.

ideas.title: Causal Disentanglement of Platform-Native Pragmatic Bias in Language Models
ideas.description: This research moves beyond descriptive adaptation to causally identify the components of platform-specific bias. It constructs controlled corpora where core content is held constant across platforms (e.g., identical news stories on Twitter, Reddit, and Facebook), but the surrounding discourse reflects native platform norms. By training diagnostic classifiers and using adversarial perturbation, the system quantifies whether LM failures stem from surface lexicon, platform-pragmatic conventions (e.g., argumentation style, humor), or structural affordances (e.g., reply trees), providing a mechanistic account of generalization failure.

ideas.title: Linguistic Mediation Analysis: Using Inverted Prediction to Uncover Demographic-Mental State Causal Pathways
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the inverted prediction approach with mechanistic investigation by using the performance and error patterns of demographic classifiers trained on mental state language as a discovery tool. By analyzing which linguistic features of mental state descriptions are most predictive of specific demographic attributes, we can hypothesize the mediating mechanisms (e.g., specific semantic frames, lexical choices, or narrative structures) through which demographics shape mental state expression. This allows for the subsequent design of causal tests, such as controlled vignette studies or longitudinal analyses, to validate whether these identified linguistic features actively mediate the relationship between demographic factors and reported mental states, thereby revealing the operational pathways of their interaction.

ideas.title: Multi-Dimensional Belief Manifold Mapping via Spectral Embedding
ideas.description: This research adapts methods from astrophysics for mapping the large-scale, multi-dimensional structure of the universe to the problem of modeling annotator subjectivity. The core analogy is that individual annotator beliefs are like galaxies distributed in a high-dimensional space of interacting belief dimensions (e.g., moral frameworks, epistemic styles, cultural contexts). The analogous solution from cosmology involves using statistical manifold learning—specifically techniques like power spectrum analysis and N-point correlation functions applied to survey data—to reconstruct the underlying 'cosmic web' from sparse observations. Adapted to this context, the research idea is to develop a framework that collects multi-dimensional belief annotations (not just task labels) and applies spectral embedding and correlation topology analysis. This would map the 'belief manifold,' identifying primary axes of variation, dense clusters of consensus, filaments of correlated subjective dimensions, and topological voids representing fundamental disagreements. The output is a structured model of the belief landscape that explains annotator behavior not as noise around a single ground truth, but as a coherent, multi-dimensional structure, enabling more nuanced data aggregation and bias understanding.

ideas.title: Adapting Delphi Method from Policy Forecasting for Prescriptive Annotation Consensus
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Delphi Method, a consensus-building technique from the field of Policy Analysis and Technological Forecasting, to solve the problem of achieving convergence on prescriptive labels among annotators. The analogous solution involves a structured, multi-round process where experts provide forecasts and reasoning, receive anonymized summaries of the group's responses, and then revise their judgments iteratively until consensus stabilizes. Adapted to the research context, a CSCW platform would be designed to facilitate this structured deliberation for annotation tasks. Annotators would not merely vote on a label but would engage in rounds of providing a label and a justification, reviewing anonymized peer labels and rationales, and then refining their own judgment. This process, guided by algorithmic facilitation that identifies areas of disagreement and highlights reasoning, systematically reduces dispersion and drives the group toward a convergent, well-reasoned prescriptive label, leveraging collective intelligence more effectively than one-off annotation or simple averaging.

ideas.title: Debate-Elicited Reasoning for Neuroatypicality Model Validation and Interpretation
ideas.description: This research direction uses structured online debate platforms to elicit detailed reasoning from a diverse pool of annotators, including those with and without neurodivergent traits, as they evaluate or generate data relevant to neuroatypicality prediction. The elicited arguments and justifications are then used as a multi-perspective benchmark to rigorously test a model's ability to predict general neuroatypicality, moving beyond diagnostic labels. This process generates a novel dataset of contrasting human reasoning patterns, which can be analyzed to identify where the model's logic aligns with or diverges from human cognitive strategies, particularly those associated with neurodivergence. The ultimate goal is to improve model interpretability, fairness, and generalizability by grounding its predictions in a spectrum of human experiential reasoning.

ideas.title: Data-Driven Discovery and Disentanglement of Orthogonal Belief Axes for Annotation
ideas.description: This research introduces a two-stage framework that first inductively discovers the fundamental, latent dimensions of annotator subjectivity from auxiliary data like rationale text, rather than pre-defining them. It applies statistical disentanglement techniques to identify a sparse set of orthogonal belief axes that explain disagreement. Second, it designs a dynamic annotation interface that presents items tailored to refine and validate these specific dimensions, resulting in a multi-dimensional belief model that is both parsimonious and grounded in observable annotator reasoning patterns.

ideas.title: Disentangling Disposition and Context: A Dynamic Latent Model of Annotator Subjectivity
ideas.description: This research proposes that annotator subjectivity arises from the interaction of separable, relatively stable personal dispositions and highly dynamic, context-dependent situational states. We develop a longitudinal annotation study where participants label the same core set of items multiple times across systematically varied contexts (e.g., different instructions, priming stimuli, or social framing). A novel hierarchical Bayesian model then disentangles variance attributable to cross-annotator dispositional factors from within-annotator state fluctuations and item-context interactions. This approach moves beyond the stable/unstable dichotomy to provide a quantifiable, predictive framework for how belief-like traits and immediate context jointly construct annotation behavior.

ideas.title: Robust Model Training via Noisy-Label Correction from Ecological Survey Data
ideas.description: This research investigates model performance when training labels are noisy self-reports rather than clinical diagnoses, drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecological surveying, species presence/absence data from citizen scientists or field observers is known to be imperfect and prone to false negatives and false positives. Ecologists solve this using occupancy-detection models that statistically separate the process of true presence from the process of detection/reporting. We adapt this solution by proposing a machine learning framework where self-reported mental health labels are treated as noisy detections of a latent clinical condition. The model would be trained to simultaneously infer the underlying 'true' diagnostic distribution and a context-dependent reporting function—parameterized by features like age, gender, culture, or survey setting—thereby de-noising the training labels and improving the reliability and fairness of the resulting diagnostic or screening tool.

ideas.title: Adversarial Debiasing with Domain Adaptation for Mental Health Representations
ideas.description: This idea adapts adversarial domain adaptation techniques from computer vision to remove demographic biases from mental health representations. In computer vision, these techniques force learned features to be invariant to nuisance domains (like lighting or weather) for robust object recognition. Here, we treat demographic groups as separate domains. The core architecture involves a shared feature extractor (e.g., a neural network processing patient language or behavioral data) whose outputs feed two networks: a primary classifier for a mental health task (e.g., predicting depression severity) and an adversarial discriminator trying to predict demographic attributes. By applying a gradient reversal layer during training, the feature extractor is optimized to improve the primary task while degrading the discriminator's performance, thereby learning demographic-invariant, clinically relevant representations. This directly transfers the proven 'invariant feature learning' solution from visual domain adaptation to the critical problem of algorithmic fairness in mental health AI.

ideas.title: Inferring a Causal Model from Observational Interventions
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from causal inference to the problem of inferring belief from prescriptive labels. In causal inference, algorithms exist to infer an underlying causal graph from observational data where known interventions have been applied. Analogously, we treat a prescriptively labeled dataset as observational data where the labels are 'interventions' enforced by a hidden belief system. The adapted method involves developing a constraint-based search algorithm that treats the prescriptive labels as fixed outputs of an unknown normative function. By analyzing the patterns in the base data that correlate with these enforced labels, the algorithm reverse-engineers the space of coherent belief systems—modeled as logical rule sets, value graphs, or probabilistic causal networks over ethical dimensions—that are consistent with the observed prescriptions. The result is a scalable, dual approach that moves from surface-level labels to the inferred latent structure of the belief system that mandated them.

ideas.title: Architectural Ablation Study: Disentangling the Components of Deep MTL for Robust Comparison
ideas.description: This research moves beyond naive end-to-end comparisons by designing a controlled experimental framework that isolates the core mechanisms of Multi-Task Learning (MTL). It systematically ablates and substitutes components (e.g., shared layers, task-specific pathways, loss weighting schemes) of a proposed deep MTL framework with functionally equivalent components from other advanced architectures, all within a unified training and evaluation protocol. This approach quantifies the marginal contribution of each architectural design choice to final performance, robustness, and data efficiency. The result is a causal understanding of what makes MTL effective for the target task, providing stronger, more generalizable insights than a simple performance leaderboard.

ideas.title: Debate-Elicited Gaussian Processes for Uncertainty in Mental Health Reasoning
ideas.description: This research integrates structured online debate platforms with Gaussian process (GP) models to create a novel framework for mental health prediction and analysis. The debate interface is used to elicit rich, contrasting rationales from clinicians or annotators for specific patient descriptions or vignettes, generating a structured corpus of reasoning. A multi-output or structured GP then models the prediction task (e.g., condition likelihood) while explicitly treating the debate-derived reasoning features as inputs, allowing the model to quantify both data uncertainty and reasoning disagreement uncertainty. The result is a predictive system that identifies not just when it is uncertain, but why—distinguishing between uncertainty due to lack of data and uncertainty stemming from genuine expert disagreement on interpretative factors, which is critical for low-prevalence or complex mental health conditions.

ideas.title: Adversarial Robustness Against Noisy Labels for Debiased Mental Health Assessment
ideas.description: This research investigates the joint application of adversarial debiasing and noisy label robustness for mental health prediction models trained on self-reported data. It develops a multi-adversarial framework where one adversary removes demographic information from the learned representations, while a second adversary identifies and down-weights potentially mislabeled training instances. This synergy allows the model to learn mental health signals that are both demographically invariant and anchored to the more reliable subset of self-reports, mitigating the compounded risks of bias amplification from noisy labels. The outcome is a more clinically trustworthy model that maintains fairness even when ground-truth clinical diagnoses are unavailable for training.

ideas.title: Dual-Track Annotation with Formal Reconciliation
ideas.description: This research designs a structured process where descriptive (objective, observational) and prescriptive (normative, goal-oriented) annotations are performed independently by separate annotator pools or in distinct phases, following strict, paradigm-specific guidelines. The outputs of these parallel tracks are then fed into a formal reconciliation module, which uses explicit rules, adjudication protocols, or computational constraints to merge them into a single, coherent label set. This approach preserves the integrity of each paradigm while systematically managing their interaction, producing hybrid data that is both philosophically sound and pragmatically useful for model training. The final output is a validated annotation framework and a dataset demonstrating the reconciliation process's efficacy.

ideas.title: Comparative Benchmarking of Deep MTL via Ecological Niche Modeling Framework
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from community ecology's niche modeling to systematically compare the proposed deep Multi-Task Learning (MTL) framework against other advanced neural architectures. In ecology, scientists map species' performance across multidimensional environmental gradients to identify their fundamental and realized niches. Analogously, we will construct a synthetic benchmarking space defined by axes of key task characteristics relevant to MTL, such as the degree of inter-task relatedness, per-task data availability, label noise levels, and task asymmetry. Within this controlled 'task space,' we will rigorously evaluate the performance of the deep MTL framework, single-task deep networks, modular networks, and other advanced architectures (e.g., cross-stitch, sluice networks). The outcome will be a detailed performance map—an 'architectural niche'—that clearly delineates the specific conditions under which the proposed deep MTL framework provides superior, equivalent, or inferior performance compared to its alternatives, moving beyond single-dataset comparisons to a principled, general understanding of its strengths and limitations.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Analysis of Demographic Moderation in Neuroatypicality Prediction
ideas.description: This research investigates the specific linguistic and causal mechanisms through which demographic attributes moderate the expression of neuroatypicality in language, as predicted by a general neuroatypicality model. It aims to determine whether the model's predictive features and their weights shift significantly across demographic groups, revealing potential biases or differential phenotypic expression. This mechanistic understanding will validate the model's robustness for broad application while explaining its limitations, ultimately contributing to more equitable and interpretable computational psychiatry tools.

ideas.title: Modeling the Linguistic Mediation of Social Positioning and Psychological Experience
ideas.description: This research investigates how language reflects and shapes psychological experience as mediated by an individual's social positioning, moving beyond static demographic categories. It employs a triangulated methodology combining large-scale corpus analysis, causal modeling of potential mediating variables (like chronic stress or community support), and in-depth collaborative interpretation with community-based researchers. The goal is to uncover the specific, context-dependent mechanisms—linguistic, situational, and structural—that link social experiences to expressed mental states, producing a dynamic, non-essentialist explanatory framework.

ideas.title: Adversarial Delphi: Structured Argumentation for Robust Prescriptive Labeling
ideas.description: This system transforms consensus-building into a structured argumentation process to avoid premature or biased agreement. Annotators are anonymously assigned to advocate for or critique potential labels in a series of reasoned exchanges, surfacing assumptions and evidence. The process iteratively aggregates these arguments and requires private reassessment, forcing convergence to be grounded in reasoning rather than social dynamics. The final output includes the agreed-upon prescriptive label alongside a documented 'dissenting rationale' to preserve critical perspectives for downstream model auditing.

ideas.title: Causal Disentanglement of Label Noise Effects via Controlled Semi-Synthetic Benchmarking
ideas.description: This research creates a controlled experimental framework to causally attribute performance differences to label noise, not model limitations. It generates or curates datasets with verified ground-truth labels (e.g., expert-adjudicated diagnoses) and then synthetically introduces noise patterns characteristic of real-world self-reports (e.g., biases, recall errors) to create paired noisy-label versions. By training and evaluating models on both the pristine and corrupted label sets while holding all other data constant, the system precisely quantifies the performance degradation directly caused by label noise. This allows for the development and testing of noise-robust learning algorithms with a known, measurable target for improvement.

ideas.title: Private reasoning journals with structured contrastive explanation
ideas.description: This system elicits descriptive annotator reasoning by decoupling it from adversarial performance. Annotators first complete a private 'reasoning journal' to capture their genuine, unpolished rationale for a data label. The system then presents an alternative, plausible reasoning path (generated from a model or another anonymized journal) and prompts the annotator to produce a structured 'contrastive explanation' identifying the key factual, inferential, or evaluative differences. This design captures the contrast between perspectives essential for understanding disagreement, but does so through a reflective, analytical task that minimizes strategic distortion of the annotator's original belief.

ideas.title: Plural: Disentangling Conflicting Beliefs from Aggregated Prescriptive Labels
ideas.description: This method treats a prescriptively labeled dataset not as the product of a monolithic belief system but as the aggregate output of multiple, potentially conflicting, normative viewpoints. It employs probabilistic graphical models or representation learning to infer a set of latent belief vectors and their respective influence weights per data point, revealing the composition of beliefs behind each label. The system can then cluster data by the dominant belief system applied, identify points of normative conflict, and reconstruct the separate belief frameworks that were merged during the labeling process.

ideas.title: Annotator Beliefs as Dynamic Contextual Constructs
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that annotator subjectivity stems from fixed, distinct personal beliefs by drawing an analogy from cognitive psychology's attribution theory. Just as attribution theory solved the problem of over-attributing behavior to personality by introducing systematic measurement of situational context, this idea adapts that solution. It proposes designing annotation experiments that systematically vary task context (instructions, priming, perceived purpose) to measure its effect on labels, hypothesizing that annotator 'beliefs' are better modeled as dynamic constructs shaped by immediate context rather than as stable, separable traits. This shifts the explanatory framework from mapping fixed annotator identities to modeling the situational vectors that influence judgment at the moment of annotation.

ideas.title: Triangulated Belief Elicitation via Behavioral Anchoring and Model-Based Debiasing
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond self-report surveys to model annotator beliefs through a multi-method framework that cross-validates and corrects for known biases. It combines structured survey instruments with behavioral tasks that financially or reputationally incentivize accurate belief expression, making implicit confidence explicit. A unifying cognitive model then integrates these disparate signals—self-reports, behavioral choices, and meta-cognitive measures—to infer a robust, debiased posterior distribution over each annotator's latent beliefs. This results in belief models that are more predictive of actual annotation behavior and less vulnerable to the artifacts of any single measurement technique.

ideas.title: Adapting Ensemble Weather Forecasting for Model Belief Plurality
ideas.description: The original idea investigates how models trained on descriptive datasets perform in applications requiring belief plurality. Drawing an analogy from Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP), which solves the analogous problem of predicting a highly uncertain future state via ensemble modeling—running multiple models with varied assumptions to generate a spread of plausible outcomes. The adapted research idea is to create an ensemble of models from a single descriptive dataset by varying architectural inductive biases, training regimes, or objective functions. The downstream performance of this ensemble would be systematically evaluated in real-world tasks (e.g., subjective Q&A, policy analysis) to see if the plurality of model 'beliefs' provides more robust, calibrated, and useful outputs than a single model, effectively quantifying and leveraging predictive uncertainty akin to weather forecasting ensembles.

ideas.title: Demographically-Stratified Belief Elicitation for Robust Mental State Inference
ideas.description: This research integrates advanced survey methodology with a rich set of demographic attributes to develop stratified belief elicitation protocols. By designing survey instruments that explicitly account for factors like socioeconomic status, cultural background, and education level, we can capture how belief distributions vary across subpopulations. This allows for the construction of mental state prediction models that disentangle demographic confounders from core cognitive or affective states. The result is a more robust, generalizable, and ethically informed framework for inferring mental states from annotated data, moving beyond simple correlation to model belief formation processes within their real-world social contexts.

ideas.title: Contextually Anchored Demographic Modeling with Provenance and Stability Tracking
ideas.description: This framework challenges static demographics by explicitly modeling their fluidity while preventing measurement collapse. It does this by separating a core, consented demographic anchor (when available and ethically sourced) from its dynamic, context-dependent expressions. All model inferences about demographic attributes are accompanied by quantified uncertainty scores and a provenance trace that logs the contextual features driving the variation. This allows for auditable fairness assessments, reproducible research comparisons, and robust systems that understand when an attribute is stable versus situational, without losing ontological grounding.

ideas.title: Adapting Radiographic Phantom Calibration for Annotator Reliability
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from medical imaging physics, this idea adapts the solution of using standardized phantoms for device calibration to the problem of annotator calibration. In medical imaging, phantoms with known structures are used to quantify scanner-specific bias and reliability. Adapted to the research context, we propose constructing a 'calibration phantom' dataset—a fixed set of items with expert-validated, gold-standard prescriptive annotations. Annotators would first label this phantom set. Their responses would then be analyzed using Item Response Theory, not to measure the annotator's ability, but to estimate two key parameters: a 'bias' parameter (modeling their systematic deviation from the gold standard on the latent trait scale) and a 'reliability' or discrimination parameter (modeling how consistently their responses relate to the item's true difficulty/controversy). These parameters would form a calibration profile for each annotator, allowing their subsequent annotations on new tasks to be weighted or adjusted, thereby calibrating out systematic bias and accounting for differential reliability in aggregated labels for prescriptive tasks.

ideas.title: Collective Norm Emergence via Deliberative Annotation
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that the source of a prescriptive belief must be external (e.g., a philosophical text or predefined rule set) by having annotators collectively define it during annotation. Drawing an analogy from evolutionary game theory and models of social norm emergence, where shared conventions arise from decentralized interactions rather than top-down imposition. The analogous solution involves populations developing stable norms through repeated interactions and learning. Adapted to this research context, the method involves small annotation groups engaging in multi-round, structured deliberation tasks. They label statements requiring normative judgment while providing rationales, then review and discuss each other's rationales across rounds. This process allows a shared, group-specific understanding of the prescriptive belief to emerge organically. The final, stabilized set of rationales and labels for that group is then recorded as the collaboratively defined 'prescriptive belief source' for that data segment, treating the emergent consensus as the operational normative framework.

ideas.title: Active Belief Plurality Engineering via Descriptive Data Conditioning
ideas.description: This research designs a system to not just audit, but actively correct, a model's inability to handle belief plurality when fine-tuned on descriptive datasets. It first constructs a diagnostic benchmark of real-world tasks requiring navigation of conflicting beliefs or values. The core intervention is a fine-tuning framework that conditions model responses on explicit belief tags or value-system identifiers extracted from the source dataset's metadata, teaching the model to generate outputs appropriate to a specified perspective. The final evaluation measures both the model's performance on pluralistic tasks and its ability to consistently align its reasoning with a given belief context upon instruction.
